\section{Index}

\vonecallout{DomSlate}{Source}{\texttt{DERIVED} \texttt{advisory\_\allowbreak{}synthesis} \texttt{not\_\allowbreak{}promoted} -\allowbreak{} Source: \texttt{docs/\allowbreak{}archive/\allowbreak{}conversations/\allowbreak{}\_\allowbreak{}wiki/\allowbreak{}decisions/\allowbreak{}index.\allowbreak{}md} -\allowbreak{} excerpted}

\section{Decisions}

Decision-\allowbreak{}like entries are extracted from archived text and are not promoted.\allowbreak{}


\subsection{Dominium Advanced Simulation and Infrastructure Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item The user then asked whether arbitrary placement was possible or whether the system was stuck to grids.\allowbreak{} The answer established that the engine should be grid-\allowbreak{}agnostic.\allowbreak{} [FACT] The user's question directly made arbitrary placement a major topic.\allowbreak{} [INFERENCE] The answer was accepted as the current design direction because the user then asked for a Codex prompt plan to implement changes necessary to achieve it.\allowbreak{}
\item Finally, the chat shifted into archival mode: it produced a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} Those later outputs preserved the discussion for future aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item [INFERENCE] The conclusion was not a finalized implementation but a working architecture: each simulation domain should be deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, content-\allowbreak{}driven, versioned, replayable, and integrated through clear read/\allowbreak{}write boundaries.\allowbreak{}
\item [INFERENCE] This was one of the strongest architecture decisions because later topics repeatedly built on it.\allowbreak{}
\item This topic matters because it defines the game's construction ambition.\allowbreak{} The engine must be flexible enough to represent real infrastructure, but the UI must not become a full CAD system.\allowbreak{}
\item The key principle was that not every system should depend on decor directly.\allowbreak{} Decorative signs and decals are cheap visual objects.\allowbreak{} If they affect simulation or gameplay, they must be promoted to devices with proper STRUCT/\allowbreak{}TRANS state and ports.\allowbreak{}
\item The final phase was preservation.\allowbreak{} The user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat report reader.\allowbreak{} These artifacts were about preserving the chat for future use and aggregation, not about new architecture decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item The chat started as a simulation architecture task.\allowbreak{} It then became a gameplay layering task.\allowbreak{} It then became an infrastructure representation task.\allowbreak{} It then became a construction UX and arbitrary placement task.\allowbreak{} Finally, it became an implementation/\allowbreak{}handoff/\allowbreak{}reporting task.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium APP0 Runtime, Platform, and Renderer Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item The central topic was APP0: the application/\allowbreak{}runtime/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}renderer layer of Dominium.\allowbreak{} This topic came up because the user provided a formal prompt defining what APP0 should do.\allowbreak{}
\item The most important conclusion was that APP0 must remain outside the core simulation rules.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} the user explicitly forbade redesigning simulation rules, altering content definitions, changing life/\allowbreak{}civilization/\allowbreak{}economy logic, and introducing gameplay shortcuts.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} authoritative logic remains in engine + game.\allowbreak{}
\item Future work must define the client's allowed dependency surface and whether it can access any simulation-\allowbreak{}adjacent prediction layer.\allowbreak{}
\item The server was defined as the authoritative runtime host.\allowbreak{} It handles simulation, sharding, scheduling, law enforcement, persistence, integrity, and anti-\allowbreak{}cheat.\allowbreak{} It must be headless-\allowbreak{}capable and support AI-\allowbreak{}only autorun and MMO scale.\allowbreak{}
\item The key conclusion is that the server should not depend on graphics or windowing.\allowbreak{} This matters because dedicated servers, cloud servers, and AI-\allowbreak{}only autorun must run without a GPU or display.\allowbreak{}
\item The key conclusion is that the launcher must not install content or alter simulation state.\allowbreak{} That belongs to setup.\allowbreak{} This separation matters because launchers often become bloated, state-\allowbreak{}mutating, or inconsistent with installer/\allowbreak{}versioning logic.\allowbreak{}
\item Tools were discussed as world inspectors, economy inspectors, replay viewers, validators, optional editors, and profilers.\allowbreak{} The important point is that tools must use the same authority rules, never bypass law, and be auditable.\allowbreak{}
\item This is a distinctive part of Dominium's philosophy.\allowbreak{} Many projects treat tools as privileged by default.\allowbreak{} This chat preserved the opposite idea: tools may need elevated abilities, but those abilities must be explicit, authorized, and logged.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture, Application Layer, TestX, and CodeHygiene Planning}

\begin{itemize}
\item A large set of prompts was then generated to lock down architecture, determinism, performance, schema governance, rendering, epistemic UI, sharding, interest sets, and fidelity projection.\allowbreak{} This became the Phase 1 hardening layer.\allowbreak{} Additional audit prompts were introduced to ensure consistency before proceeding into life, civilization, war, content, agents, tools, mods, and final long-\allowbreak{}term policy.\allowbreak{}
\item Because the chat was huge, the user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet.\allowbreak{} Then they asked for downloadable report files.\allowbreak{} Then they asked for an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader.\allowbreak{} Finally, they asked for this human-\allowbreak{}readable explanatory report.\allowbreak{} This final report is meant to let a future assistant or human understand the substance without re-\allowbreak{}reading the whole conversation.\allowbreak{}
\item The central project is a deterministic universe engine/\allowbreak{}game.\allowbreak{} The user's ambition is not just a normal game but a world runtime that can represent everything from a single-\allowbreak{}room scenario to an AI-\allowbreak{}only universe to an MMO.\allowbreak{} The project is meant to support real-\allowbreak{}world defaults, arbitrary modding, procedural and defined content, strong epistemics, and long-\allowbreak{}term replayability.\allowbreak{}
\item The user provided a canonical repository structure and non-\allowbreak{}negotiable engine/\allowbreak{}game dependency direction.\allowbreak{} The engine is reusable and independent.\allowbreak{} The game depends on engine public APIs only.\allowbreak{} Engine internals must not leak into game/\allowbreak{}tools.\allowbreak{} Rendering backends belong to the engine, not the client.\allowbreak{} Launcher/\allowbreak{}setup logic must not enter engine/\allowbreak{}game.\allowbreak{} Client/\allowbreak{}server are executables that bind engine and game.\allowbreak{}
\item This topic is central to long-\allowbreak{}term scalability.\allowbreak{} It allows future CPUs, GPUs, NPUs, heterogeneous cores, cache structures, and distributed servers to be supported by adding backend policies rather than rewriting gameplay.\allowbreak{}
\item Rendering decisions were framed around explicit API first: Vulkan/\allowbreak{}DX12/\allowbreak{}Metal inform core contracts; legacy renderers are sinks or downgraded consumers.\allowbreak{} Rendering never affects authoritative simulation.\allowbreak{}
\item The user also wanted AI-\allowbreak{}only civilizations that could exist outside bounds of play but become visitable and indistinguishable from real civilizations.\allowbreak{} The answer was existence states and refinement contracts.\allowbreak{} A macro civilization can be alive without micro-\allowbreak{}simulating every citizen, but if it is reachable and visitable, it must refine deterministically into micro detail consistent with its macro history.\allowbreak{}
\item Large prompt families were generated for life/\allowbreak{}death/\allowbreak{}continuity, civilization/\allowbreak{}economy/\allowbreak{}governance, war/\allowbreak{}conflict, canonical content, agents, tools, mods, and final long-\allowbreak{}term maintenance.\allowbreak{} These are extensive background artifacts.\allowbreak{} Later, the chat shifted away from implementing those systems here because another bootstrap declared the core design complete and locked.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium/Domino Architecture and Codex Prompt Roadmap}

\begin{itemize}
\item The most important thing to remember is this: \textbf{this chat is the architectural and implementation-roadmap backbone for Dominium/Domino, but it is not an implementation log}.\allowbreak{} It should be preserved as a design/\allowbreak{}specification source and as a prompt library, while actual code and current facts must be verified separately.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user opened by pasting an "Extended Master Starter Prompt -\allowbreak{} Dominium + Domino.\allowbreak{}" That prompt established the role of the assistant as a senior engine architect and defined the main project philosophy.\allowbreak{} Domino was described as a deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, C89 engine portable across old and modern OS/\allowbreak{}architecture combinations.\allowbreak{} Dominium was described as the product suite built on top of Domino.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user asked, succinctly, how a player would build a complete machine workshop.\allowbreak{} The assistant described a progression: extract raw materials, process them into industrial materials, produce mechanical components, produce electrical components, establish power distribution, establish logistics, assemble machine frames and machines, add measurement/\allowbreak{}control tooling, then scale horizontally and vertically.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user then said they wanted inserters and belts, but "much more realistic" and with "WAY less processing power.\allowbreak{}" They wanted conveyors/\allowbreak{}inserters to make sense in real-\allowbreak{}world use cases, and suggested inserters might be more generic robot arms.\allowbreak{} They also wanted belts constructed as splines like roads and rails.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user explicitly wanted packaging into crates, bags, pallets, containers, jars, cans, boxes, etc.\allowbreak{} The stated purpose was to reduce the number of physical items simulated while preserving realistic packing/\allowbreak{}unpacking mechanics and obeying mass, volume, density, and other physical constraints.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user then asked how hydrology, atmology, lithology, light, rain, wind, temperature, pressure, pollution, magnetic fields, radiation, gases, and similar phenomena should work with buildings and enclosed spaces.\allowbreak{} They also asked how to support blueprints/\allowbreak{}templates and how to build realistically on sloped terrain without ugly foundation blocks or forced leveling.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user asked whether the whole system could be made more extensible and modular.\allowbreak{} The assistant proposed applying the same pattern everywhere: core + registry + models + data.\allowbreak{} This produced the repeated architecture model used later in prompts: each subsystem has a core API, model family/\allowbreak{}vtables, data prototypes, runtime instances, registries, TLV schemas, and save/\allowbreak{}load hooks.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: The user asked to summarize the entire system and then asked for the hierarchy of data definitions/\allowbreak{}types and how to implement actual items/\allowbreak{}functions/\allowbreak{}features.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced major architectural summaries: IDs, protos, models, instances, systems; resource/\allowbreak{}env/\allowbreak{}build/\allowbreak{}trans/\allowbreak{}items/\allowbreak{}struct/\allowbreak{}vehicle/\allowbreak{}blueprints/\allowbreak{}jobs/\allowbreak{}worldgen/\allowbreak{}save.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture, UI, Providers, and Robot OS Strategy}

\begin{itemize}
\item The language baseline decision matters because it changes what old renderers and toolchains matter.\allowbreak{} C17/\allowbreak{}C++17 reduces the need to design around C89/\allowbreak{}C++98 constraints but does not remove the need for C-\allowbreak{}compatible ABI boundaries.\allowbreak{} The system floor decision similarly moves many older renderers and OS targets into research/\allowbreak{}back-\allowbreak{}port categories.\allowbreak{}
\item The Robot OS and robotic seed-\allowbreak{}civilisation decisions are the most important design decisions.\allowbreak{} They are explicit user statements, not merely assistant proposals.\allowbreak{} They should be formalized as product/\allowbreak{}game requirements.\allowbreak{} They affect UI, fog-\allowbreak{}of-\allowbreak{}war, spawning, respawn, automation, industry, tutorial/\allowbreak{}planner design, and MMO anti-\allowbreak{}cheat.\allowbreak{}
\item The central tradeoff was speed versus purity.\allowbreak{} Raylib, SDL2, Lua, raygui, rlgl, rlsw, and related libraries can accelerate visible progress, but if they leak into game law, UI documents, saves, replays, or public contracts, they become architectural capture.\allowbreak{} The chosen compromise is to use them as providers behind Dominium-\allowbreak{}owned services and conformance tests.\allowbreak{}
\item The final game direction is robotic seed civilisation, not labour management.\allowbreak{}
\item 2.\allowbreak{} "Summarize the final renderer plan after the C17/\allowbreak{}C++17 transition.\allowbreak{}"
\item 4.\allowbreak{} "Which decisions in this chat were explicit user decisions versus assistant synthesis?"
\item 5.\allowbreak{} "Which renderer/\allowbreak{}platform decisions are final, and which are still provisional?"
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Build and Future-Proofing Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item Plain-\allowbreak{}language limitation: this package is reliable as a preservation of the current visible chat.\allowbreak{} It should not be treated as a complete archive of every Dominium discussion in other chats.\allowbreak{} Where the report uses project or repository context, it is labelled as such.\allowbreak{} The most important caveat is that many items are recommendations, not final user decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item The final uploaded prompt requested a complete preservation package for this chat: a human-\allowbreak{}readable explanation, structured registers, context transfer packet, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, and files/\allowbreak{}ZIP package.\allowbreak{} This response completes that export task and creates downloadable files for later reading and aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item The final explicit goal was preservation: create a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity package for this chat so the user can understand it later, ask questions in this chat, merge it with other old-\allowbreak{}chat reports, and eventually build a master project spec book.\allowbreak{}
\item The most important caution is that only DECISION-\allowbreak{}01 is clearly a user-\allowbreak{}stated constraint in this visible chat.\allowbreak{} The other items are strong recommendations produced in response to the user's requests for best practice; they should not be merged into canon unless the user later accepts them.\allowbreak{}
\item Manual drag-\allowbreak{}and-\allowbreak{}drop repo moves were rejected in earlier context and preserved here as a general risk; moves must go through migration maps, validators, shims, and proof.\allowbreak{}
\item 10.\allowbreak{} \textbf{Feed this report into the master spec book aggregator.} Preserve uncertainty labels and avoid merging suggestions as decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item Determinism, fixed-\allowbreak{}point behavior, and no hidden behavior are central constraints.\allowbreak{}
\item Multi-\allowbreak{}floor builds must be separate artifacts.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Canonical Architecture, Repository Foundation, and Provider Model}

\begin{itemize}
\item This produced a pattern: generate a large Codex/\allowbreak{}AIDE task, the user ran it or reported a result, then the assistant evaluated what was truly fixed versus what was only validator/\allowbreak{}document churn.\allowbreak{} The user eventually demanded a one-\allowbreak{}shot "actual final cleanup" prompt because previous passes had sometimes added validators without moving directories.\allowbreak{} That prompt explicitly required real \texttt{git mv} routing, not just reports.\allowbreak{}
\item The final provider directory doctrine became service-\allowbreak{}first: \texttt{runtime/\allowbreak{}<service>/\allowbreak{}providers/\allowbreak{}<provider>/\allowbreak{}}, not \texttt{runtime/\allowbreak{}<vendor>/\allowbreak{}<service>/\allowbreak{}}.\allowbreak{} Provider choices belong in \texttt{release/\allowbreak{}profiles/\allowbreak{}} or \texttt{content/\allowbreak{}profiles/\allowbreak{}}, not app path names.\allowbreak{} External code belongs under \texttt{external/\allowbreak{}upstream/\allowbreak{}} or \texttt{external/\allowbreak{}vendor/\allowbreak{}}, but the repo should choose one convention.\allowbreak{}
\item Packs are distributable authored payloads.\allowbreak{} The preferred pack layout became category-\allowbreak{}based, such as \texttt{content/\allowbreak{}packs/\allowbreak{}<category>/\allowbreak{}<pack\_\allowbreak{}id>/\allowbreak{}}.\allowbreak{} Pack IDs must not depend on paths.\allowbreak{} Saves and replays must use stable IDs, schema versions, canonical serialization, deterministic hashes, and explicit migration/\allowbreak{}refusal policies.\allowbreak{} Backward compatibility must be a compatibility corpus plus tests, not intention.\allowbreak{}
\item INFERENCE: The user wanted a durable method for deciding future structure changes, not just a one-\allowbreak{}time layout.\allowbreak{} They also wanted the assistant to become more rigorous: distinguish decisions from brainstorms, stop over-\allowbreak{}planning without execution, and preserve uncertainty.\allowbreak{} The user also wanted a structure that supports other Domino-\allowbreak{}based games and possibly different engine implementations.\allowbreak{}
\item The closed root model was the most emotionally and technically important decision.\allowbreak{} The user repeatedly objected to root proliferation; the final root set became the anchor for all later advice.\allowbreak{} This decision affects every future proposal: if a concept can fit under an existing root, it should not become a new top-\allowbreak{}level root.\allowbreak{}
\item The no-\allowbreak{}\texttt{src} rule was also critical.\allowbreak{} The user considered repeated \texttt{src/\allowbreak{}} and \texttt{source/\allowbreak{}} folders as a central symptom of bad architecture.\allowbreak{} The final doctrine is that ownership directories are already source roots; implementation should live under meaningful module/\allowbreak{}subsystem folders, not generic \texttt{src} wrappers.\allowbreak{}
\item The framework-\allowbreak{}boundary decision rejected a top-\allowbreak{}level \texttt{framework/\allowbreak{}} root.\allowbreak{} The accepted model is that Domino Framework exists as public surfaces, ABI headers, contracts, and provider/\allowbreak{}service law.\allowbreak{} This prevents root sprawl while still supporting SDK/\allowbreak{}export concepts later.\allowbreak{}
\item The provider model decision was a major response to raylib/\allowbreak{}SDL/\allowbreak{}Lua discussions.\allowbreak{} Third-\allowbreak{}party libraries should be providers behind first-\allowbreak{}party service contracts.\allowbreak{} They can accelerate visible progress but must not own saved data, game law, public ABI, or content schemas.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Chronology \& Celestial Systems}

\begin{itemize}
\item This reinforced the core celestial-\allowbreak{}content rule: locations should exist explicitly when they change player decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item This became the spatial foundation for later time/\allowbreak{}calendar decisions because every major body or region could potentially have its own local time, calendar, or operational standard.\allowbreak{}
\item The assistant formalized this as the Perfect Earth Calendar, later called HPC-\allowbreak{}E.\allowbreak{} The final structure is clear, though the exact leap rule remains unresolved.\allowbreak{}
\item This material is useful but less final than the Earth calendar.\allowbreak{} The user asked to apply the same approach broadly, but did not individually confirm every name or number.\allowbreak{} The final package correctly marks these as needing review.\allowbreak{}
\item The final state is that this chat became both a design discussion and a packaged source document for future aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item The conclusion was not "simulate everything.\allowbreak{}" The conclusion was to classify content by relevance.\allowbreak{} Explicit objects are justified when they create gameplay, physics, navigation, history, strategy, or meaningful decisions.\allowbreak{} Otherwise, they should be procedural or statistical.\allowbreak{}
\item What remains uncertain is the exact verified object list.\allowbreak{} The assistant generated a real Milky Way system list, but that list must be checked before becoming data.\allowbreak{}
\item The conclusion was that every explicit celestial feature should change player decisions in at least one way.\allowbreak{} If it does not, it probably should not be explicit.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Development Routes and Continuity Preservation}

\begin{itemize}
\item Before relying on this chat as project authority, a future assistant or human reviewer must confirm whether Dominium is actually intended to be simulation-\allowbreak{}heavy, whether strict determinism matters, whether replay or multiplayer are goals, whether modding matters, and what the actual game loop is.\allowbreak{} Until then, the kernel-\allowbreak{}first plan should be treated as a strong provisional proposal, not a final specification.\allowbreak{}
\item The first answer was assertive.\allowbreak{} It stated that Dominium must follow Route C and described the route as the only viable one for Dominium.\allowbreak{} Later parts of the chat corrected the status of that claim.\allowbreak{} FACT: the assistant made that recommendation.\allowbreak{} UNCERTAIN /\allowbreak{} UNVERIFIED: the recommendation was not validated with external sources in the chat and was not explicitly accepted by the user.\allowbreak{}
\item The initial technical answer proposed a layered stack.\allowbreak{} It started with mathematical and temporal primitives, then moved into the deterministic simulation kernel, then world state, systems, persistence and replay, tooling, presentation, content, and finally distribution, modding, and multiplayer.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then asked to turn the Context Transfer Packet and visible chat context into a final downloadable, shareable, reusable report package.\allowbreak{} This was a packaging and normalization task.\allowbreak{} The user specified exact output files: a full chat report, YAML spec sheet, aggregator packet, registers, reader brief, verification/\allowbreak{}audit file, manifest, and ZIP package.\allowbreak{}
\item After the downloadable package was generated, the user asked not to create another handoff package but to render the package contents into a readable, navigable in-\allowbreak{}chat report.\allowbreak{} This led to an "IN-\allowbreak{}CHAT REPORT READER" response.\allowbreak{} That response explained the package contents, the workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, artifacts, risks, verification queue, and next questions.\allowbreak{}
\item The final state of the conversation is therefore: the chat has produced an architectural proposal for Dominium, converted that proposal into preservation artifacts, rendered those artifacts back into an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader format, and now requires a plain-\allowbreak{}English briefing that explains the substance and significance of the conversation.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: these three routes were discussed.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT: Route C was recommended by the assistant.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Documentation Standards, README Strategy, and Handoff Packaging}

\begin{itemize}
\item The key thing to remember: this chat produced the standards and prompts for future work; it did not verify or change the project.\allowbreak{} The next assistant must inspect the actual repository before writing repo-\allowbreak{}specific docs, README content, platform claims, license claims, or subsystem descriptions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{FACT:} The chat established that public API contracts should live in headers.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{FACT:} Source files should document implementation rationale, non-\allowbreak{}obvious invariants, and hazards.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{FACT:} External docs should cover architecture, dependency rules, extension recipes, and subsystem-\allowbreak{}level explanations.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{FACT:} The examples were generated in the chat.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{FACT:} They were not claimed to exist in the repository.\allowbreak{}
\item This was a major decision: README should not be the full spec.\allowbreak{} It should orient readers and point them to the deeper docs.\allowbreak{}
\item The final phase of the chat moved away from project documentation and into preserving the chat itself.\allowbreak{} The user requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity Context Transfer Packet, then requested that it be turned into a final downloadable/\allowbreak{}shareable report package.\allowbreak{} That package was created and included a full report, YAML spec sheet, aggregator packet, registers, reader brief, verification/\allowbreak{}audit file, manifest, and ZIP archive.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture I}

\begin{itemize}
\item During this stage, the work was still broadly about "the project spec.\allowbreak{}" The user wanted a comprehensive description of the game and its technical systems.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced large design documents, but many of those earlier contents are not visible in the retained transcript.\allowbreak{} This is important because the final report package marks those earlier outputs as referenced but not fully recovered.\allowbreak{}
\item The user also made a clear versioning decision.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wanted \textbf{major.minor.patch semantic versioning} for all components/\allowbreak{}packages and for the complete game package.\allowbreak{} The user explicitly did \textbf{not} want build numbers or build dates in filenames.\allowbreak{} Instead, those details should live in metadata.\allowbreak{} This became part of the future build/\allowbreak{}package design.\allowbreak{}
\item The key change came when the user decided that Codex could not read the entire conversation at once.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user said they would copy and paste the transcript with timestamps and would not edit it manually, but because Codex could not read the whole context at once, they would not bother asking Codex to compile a full book directly from the whole chat.\allowbreak{}
\item The user provided a large \texttt{devspec/\allowbreak{}} file tree.\allowbreak{} It included top-\allowbreak{}level docs, engine, platform, render, audio, systems, game, UI, launcher, mods, tools, data, tests, and scripts.\allowbreak{} Then the user changed one important thing: \textbf{FACT:} they decided to skip the top-\allowbreak{}level \texttt{.\allowbreak{}txt} files because the original Markdown docs already existed in \texttt{/\allowbreak{}docs/\allowbreak{}.\allowbreak{}.\allowbreak{}.\allowbreak{}}.\allowbreak{}
\item The game layer began with \texttt{g\_\allowbreak{}core.\allowbreak{}h}, \texttt{g\_\allowbreak{}core.\allowbreak{}cpp}, and \texttt{g\_\allowbreak{}world.\allowbreak{}h}.\allowbreak{} The user then shifted to handoff/\allowbreak{}report work before \texttt{g\_\allowbreak{}world.\allowbreak{}cpp} was produced.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The next strict-\allowbreak{}order file is therefore \texttt{game/\allowbreak{}g\_\allowbreak{}world.\allowbreak{}cpp.\allowbreak{}txt}.\allowbreak{}
\item The final generated package contains seven report files and a ZIP archive.\allowbreak{} The latest user request asks for a human-\allowbreak{}readable explanation rather than another machine-\allowbreak{}readable packet.\allowbreak{} This current report is therefore the narrative version: it explains what happened, what mattered, and what should be remembered.\allowbreak{}
\item FACT:** The whole chat revolves around the Dominium Game project.\allowbreak{} Dominium is described through its architecture: a deterministic, cross-\allowbreak{}platform simulation game with industrial, economic, logistics, climate, terrain, AI, research, and modding systems.\allowbreak{} The user was not merely asking for isolated code snippets.\allowbreak{} They were building a full software architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item The most important conclusion is that determinism is central.\allowbreak{} Systems are repeatedly specified to avoid hidden randomness, OS timers, platform dependence, uncontrolled floating point, and runtime allocation during simulation ticks.\allowbreak{} The visible rationale is that the project needs replay, save/\allowbreak{}load, and likely lockstep multiplayer compatibility.\allowbreak{} This requirement affects nearly every generated spec.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture II}

\begin{itemize}
\item The last part of the chat was handoff extraction.\allowbreak{} The user asked for discovery inventory, structured registers, a context transfer packet, and then a downloadable package.\allowbreak{} The generated package captured the chat's workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, artifacts, risks, and verification items.\allowbreak{} This current report is a plain-\allowbreak{}language explanation of that substance.\allowbreak{}
\item The entire conversation is anchored by the requirement that Dominium must run deterministically.\allowbreak{} This means the same initial state and same inputs must produce the same state across machines, compilers, operating systems, and eventually retro and modern platforms.\allowbreak{}
\item The chat discussed fixed tick phases, virtual lanes, merge order, command buffers, event queues, and deterministic serialization.\allowbreak{} The simulation must not depend on render FPS, wall-\allowbreak{}clock time, OS scheduler behaviour, GPU state, or floating-\allowbreak{}point quirks.\allowbreak{} This explains many later decisions: C89 core, integer/\allowbreak{}fixed-\allowbreak{}point math, no sim floats, no platform headers in simulation, and renderer as pure observer.\allowbreak{}
\item What remains uncertain is the exact code-\allowbreak{}level API and component layouts.\allowbreak{} The chat produced specs and prompts, not final source code.\allowbreak{}
\item The decision to use on-\allowbreak{}rails orbital mechanics mattered because it avoids the complexity of full n-\allowbreak{}body simulation.\allowbreak{} Instead of integrating every orbital body continuously, the game can use deterministic graph-\allowbreak{}like orbital states and transitions.\allowbreak{} This fits the rest of the engine: graphs, fields, frames, and state machines.\allowbreak{}
\item Railways began as a major topic but later became part of the larger transport framework.\allowbreak{} Roads, waterways, airways, and spaceways were all discussed.\allowbreak{} The final architecture treats transport as graph/\allowbreak{}corridor/\allowbreak{}path systems.\allowbreak{} Rail and road use alignments and nodes.\allowbreak{} Water can use depth fields plus navigation graphs.\allowbreak{} Air can use flight corridors, runways, and holding patterns.\allowbreak{} Space uses orbital graphs.\allowbreak{}
\item The final portion of the chat created a formal package of reports: full chat report, YAML spec sheet, aggregator packet, registers, reader brief, audit file, and manifest.\allowbreak{} These are not design decisions themselves, but they are important artifacts for later Project aggregation.\allowbreak{} The current response exists because the user wanted a human-\allowbreak{}readable explanation of the chat rather than more machine-\allowbreak{}readable outputs.\allowbreak{}
\item The building-\allowbreak{}system goal also evolved.\allowbreak{} The user originally asked vector versus voxel.\allowbreak{} The final model became hybrid: discrete blocks/\allowbreak{}cells/\allowbreak{}faces/\allowbreak{}edges as sim truth, vector as authoring/\allowbreak{}generation/\allowbreak{}visual layer.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture III: Launcher, Platform, Renderer, and Handoff Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item The view system then expanded beyond the launcher.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wants instant zoom to any scale, instant switching between top-\allowbreak{}down 2D and first-\allowbreak{}person 3D, and arbitrary cameras for free cam, map viewing, content creation, HUD cameras, CCTV, overlays, windows, and offscreen targets.\allowbreak{} These are client/\allowbreak{}render-\allowbreak{}side concerns, not simulation concerns.\allowbreak{}
\item This context matters because the rest of the conversation happened inside those constraints.\allowbreak{} The launcher could not become a dumping ground for OS-\allowbreak{}specific behavior, sim mutation, renderer decisions, or nondeterministic game-\allowbreak{}state changes.\allowbreak{} It had to fit the project's deterministic layering.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then uploaded \texttt{dominium.\allowbreak{}7z}, saying it was the git repository as of that moment.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The prior assistant said it could not open \texttt{.\allowbreak{}7z} archives in that environment.\allowbreak{} That means the actual repository state remained unverified at that stage.\allowbreak{} This became important later, because many implementation ideas were discussed without confirmed access to the repo contents.\allowbreak{}
\item The user clarified: \textbf{FACT:} "The settings selected in the launcher will be used to execute client or server binaries.\allowbreak{}"
\item The user then specified another important constraint: \textbf{FACT:} the launcher backend should be C89, while frontend code for each binary can use C++98.\allowbreak{} The user also said client/\allowbreak{}server binaries can have single-\allowbreak{}system support.\allowbreak{}
\item A \textbf{C89 launcher backend/core} owns logical decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item The final direction became:
\item The conversation then refined the renderer design.\allowbreak{} The assistant had discussed \texttt{vector\_\allowbreak{}soft}, but the user rejected the idea of having a separate vector-\allowbreak{}only renderer.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user asked whether instead Dominium could have "just software renderers" capable of vector graphics and graphics using texture files on all systems as a fallback.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture IV}

\begin{itemize}
\item The reusable engine layer was named \textbf{Domino}.\allowbreak{} This became one of the most important naming and architectural decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} Domino is the engine/\allowbreak{}core/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}sim/\allowbreak{}mod layer, reusable for other games and projects.\allowbreak{} Dominium is the game/\allowbreak{}product layer built on it.\allowbreak{}
\item Blueprints are plans or diffs: place element, remove element, modify terrain, place machine, connect network, etc.\allowbreak{} When accepted, they generate jobs.\allowbreak{} Manual player actions and queued work for humans, robots, or other agents should use the same job pipeline.\allowbreak{} This matters because it keeps replay, AI, and automation consistent.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then asked how to structure Codex prompts.\allowbreak{} The final high-\allowbreak{}level roadmap became:
\item The assistant generated detailed Phase 1 prompts, platform backend templates, renderer backend templates, setup prompts, launcher prompts, tools prompts, and finally a Phase 7+ roadmap for the game.\allowbreak{} The game phase itself was not expanded into the same full prompt detail as Phases 1-\allowbreak{}6.\allowbreak{} That remains a future task.\allowbreak{}
\item At the end, the user shifted from architecture planning to chat retirement.\allowbreak{} The chat produced a discovery inventory, a context transfer packet, a final downloadable report package, and then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} The current request asks for a human-\allowbreak{}readable narrative explanation rather than more registers or machine-\allowbreak{}oriented handoff files.\allowbreak{}
\item The conclusion was clear and accepted: \textbf{Domino is the reusable engine; Dominium is the game/product layer}.\allowbreak{} What remains uncertain is the actual repository state.\allowbreak{} The chat planned the split, but did not verify whether the repo already matches it.\allowbreak{}
\item The conclusion was to define UI modes separately from UI backends.\allowbreak{} This is one of the central product architecture decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item The package/\allowbreak{}instance system is central to the whole ecosystem.\allowbreak{} The user wants installed versions, official base/\allowbreak{}DLC/\allowbreak{}mod content, downloaded third-\allowbreak{}party mods and packs, shared data, instance-\allowbreak{}specific overrides, and arbitrary combinations without duplicating every possible mix.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Canon, Repository Alignment, and Portability Doctrine}

\begin{itemize}
\item This chat was about preserving and re-\allowbreak{}grounding Dominium's architecture around a very old constitutional contract, then checking whether the current GitHub repository still follows that contract, and finally broadening the question into a platform-\allowbreak{}engineering doctrine for portability, modularity, extensibility, reuse, future-\allowbreak{}proofing, and long-\allowbreak{}term compatibility.\allowbreak{}
\item The glossary bound terms such as Authority, Law, Process, Lens, SessionSpec, UniverseIdentity, Macro Capsule, SRZ, RepoX, TestX, AuditX, CompatX, SecureX, and related terms.\allowbreak{} It matters because future assistants must not use sloppy synonyms like "mode" where the canon requires ExperienceProfile or LawProfile.\allowbreak{} This topic directly supports modularity because stable vocabulary is part of stable architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item Uncertainty: during this preservation pass, a potential inconsistency appeared: some docs claim product roots moved under \texttt{apps/\allowbreak{}}, while an earlier inspected code path under root \texttt{client/\allowbreak{}} was available and \texttt{apps/\allowbreak{}client} was not fetched successfully.\allowbreak{} This must be verified against the current physical tree.\allowbreak{}
\item DECISION-\allowbreak{}01:** The old v1 contract/\allowbreak{}glossary were accepted as the baseline for this chat because the user supplied them as the old ground truth and asked later questions against them.\allowbreak{}
\item DECISION-\allowbreak{}02:** The live repo audit refined authority: the repo-\allowbreak{}materialized canon files and canon index are stronger for current repository work than raw prompts.\allowbreak{}
\item DECISION-\allowbreak{}03:** Ownership-\allowbreak{}root layout was preferred because it encodes who owns a file or subsystem, while generic \texttt{src}/\allowbreak{}\texttt{source} hides responsibility.\allowbreak{}
\item DECISION-\allowbreak{}04:** Stable contracts plus replaceable implementations best satisfy the user's desire for rewrites/\allowbreak{}refactors without losing compatibility.\allowbreak{}
\item DECISION-\allowbreak{}05:** The repo should not be described as fully implementing the old v1 vision yet; many systems are still declarative, scaffolded, or deferred.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium + Domino Codex Planning and Handoff}

\begin{itemize}
\item INFERENCE:** The user accepted this direction in practice, because the next request was to generate Codex prompts to implement each step fully.\allowbreak{} The user did not explicitly say, "I formally approve the Milestone-\allowbreak{}0 plan," but they proceeded to ask for prompts based on it.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then asked for recommendations.\allowbreak{} The assistant recommended a clean startup policy: explicit flags first, no environment/\allowbreak{}config override in v1, terminal detection through dsys, generic AUTO behavior, product-\allowbreak{}specific headless handling for the game, build-\allowbreak{}time GUI/\allowbreak{}TUI capabilities, and structural error codes for fallback.\allowbreak{} The user accepted this enough to request a "one big Codex prompt" to implement it.\allowbreak{}
\item However, the later context transfer packet treated that inspection claim as \textbf{UNCERTAIN / UNVERIFIED}.\allowbreak{} This was careful and correct: within the visible chat, there was no reliable tool trace proving the repo had actually been inspected.\allowbreak{} Therefore, future assistants must verify \texttt{dominium.\allowbreak{}zip} or the active checkout before relying on the platform/\allowbreak{}render/\allowbreak{}API answer.\allowbreak{}
\item The core topic was the architecture of the Dominium + Domino project.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user defined Domino as a deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, C89 engine layer and Dominium as a C++98 product suite.\allowbreak{} This matters because every implementation decision had to respect those boundaries.\allowbreak{}
\item The conclusions were clear: future code must not bypass dsys or dgfx, deterministic sim must not use host floating-\allowbreak{}point, product code should not directly call OS APIs, and old content must not silently break.\allowbreak{} These are not just preferences; they are the governing rules of the project.\allowbreak{}
\item The numeric core was central because deterministic simulation depends on it.\allowbreak{} The generated prompt specified fixed-\allowbreak{}point types, deterministic RNG, saturating arithmetic, and a numeric test harness.\allowbreak{}
\item The unresolved complication is C89 portability.\allowbreak{} Standard C89 does not provide \texttt{long long}, but \texttt{u64}, \texttt{i64}, and \texttt{q48\_\allowbreak{}16} require 64-\allowbreak{}bit representation.\allowbreak{} Future work must decide whether to allow compiler extensions, emulate 64-\allowbreak{}bit values for strict retro targets, or define platform tiers with conditional support.\allowbreak{}
\item Prompt 4 translated this into a minimal repo manifest system.\allowbreak{} It proposed product manifests, compatibility profile fields, and a launcher path that loads a primary game product.\allowbreak{} This was important because the launcher-\allowbreak{}>game relationship is central to Dominium as a product suite.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Workbench, AIDE, Presentation Architecture, Provider Strategy, Product-Spine Planning, and Preservation Companion}

\begin{itemize}
\item A future assistant must understand that this chat is not simply about UI.\allowbreak{} It records the architecture for how Dominium should build itself: through contracts, commands, services, providers, documents, patches, modules, packs, apps, workspaces, diagnostics, evidence, AIDE, Codex, and eventually Workbench.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then redirected the entire plan.\allowbreak{} They argued that native OS widget GUI tools already exist through Visual Studio, Xcode, etc.\allowbreak{} The project needed a cross-\allowbreak{}platform rendered tool environment that uses the same CLI, TUI, and rendered GUI systems as the client.\allowbreak{} The old UI Editor and Tool Editor were good exploratory ideas but bad final products.\allowbreak{} This produced the Dominium Workbench concept.\allowbreak{}
\item The discussion then moved into AIDE.\allowbreak{} The user wanted to automate as much as possible through Codex and AIDE.\allowbreak{} We developed a task operating model: AIDE creates WorkUnits, tracks attempts, blockers, evidence, repairs, resumes, checkpoints, and promotion decisions.\allowbreak{} Codex executes bounded tasks.\allowbreak{} Development can continue with classified partials and repairable blockers; promotion to main requires evidence.\allowbreak{}
\item The final part of the conversation dealt with repo status and task queue.\allowbreak{} The user pasted status reports indicating that \texttt{PRESENTATION-\allowbreak{}CONTRACT-\allowbreak{}01} completed with warnings, and then chose to generate six maintenance prompts before replanning.\allowbreak{} \texttt{FULL-\allowbreak{}GATE-\allowbreak{}LEGACY-\allowbreak{}TEST-\allowbreak{}ROUTE-\allowbreak{}01} was generated.\allowbreak{} This preservation task followed.\allowbreak{}
\item The early UI Editor plan was a structured attempt to fix the launcher UI and build a tool for UI authoring.\allowbreak{} It covered DUI/\allowbreak{}TLV, native widgets, Win32 preview, deterministic TLV/\allowbreak{}JSON, layout, validation, codegen, \texttt{ops.\allowbreak{}json}, import/\allowbreak{}export, and launcher/\allowbreak{}setup generation.\allowbreak{} The conclusion was that these are useful components but not the final product architecture.\allowbreak{} They should become Workbench modules and services.\allowbreak{}
\item Workbench became the central product concept.\allowbreak{} It is a cross-\allowbreak{}platform rendered production environment, not a monolithic editor.\allowbreak{} It hosts workspaces and modules over contracts, commands, documents, patches, services, providers, packs, artifacts, evidence, and tests.\allowbreak{} It eventually self-\allowbreak{}hosts by editing its own safe artifacts first, then product UIs, packs, modules, apps, and AIDE work units.\allowbreak{}
\item The rendered GUI must be backend-\allowbreak{}agnostic.\allowbreak{} Layouts, controls, widgets, themes, styles, views, and workspaces should be modular and extensible.\allowbreak{} Themes include first-\allowbreak{}party primitive-\allowbreak{}only profiles and OEM+ mimic profiles.\allowbreak{} The GUI should be task-\allowbreak{}first, command-\allowbreak{}backed, evidence-\allowbreak{}visible, and projection-\allowbreak{}neutral.\allowbreak{}
\item Preserve all decisions, tasks, prompts, constraints, files, and artifacts in a downloadable package.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Setup Architecture and Handoff}

\begin{itemize}
\item Near the end, the chat shifted into preservation mode.\allowbreak{} The user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then for a downloadable report package, then for an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version of that package.\allowbreak{} Those outputs preserved the decisions, tasks, constraints, artifacts, rejected options, and verification queue for future assistants or aggregation into a larger Project Spec Book.\allowbreak{}

\subsection{Dominium APP0 Runtime, Platform, and Renderer Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item What remains uncertain is whether the current repository already enforces these boundaries mechanically.\allowbreak{} The actual code needs inspection.\allowbreak{}
\item The unresolved issue is sharding semantics.\allowbreak{} APP0 requires sharding hooks, but the chat did not decide whether shards are spatial, logical, temporal, jurisdictional, or something else.\allowbreak{} The report preserved the idea that APP0 can provide sharding plumbing without inventing simulation semantics.\allowbreak{}
\item The unresolved issue is the trust and integrity model for setup.\allowbreak{} The chat did not decide whether content verification should use hashes, signatures, local manifests, network verification, or another model.\allowbreak{}
\item The unresolved issue is the exact elevation/\allowbreak{}audit model.\allowbreak{} The chat did not decide how tools authenticate, request write permissions, log actions, or interact with law enforcement modules.\allowbreak{}
\item The unresolved issue is the exact renderer capability schema.\allowbreak{} The chat proposed ideas but did not finalize fields, priorities, or target matrices.\allowbreak{}
\item The user asked to look at the old code snapshot in project attachments.\allowbreak{} A prior assistant claimed to inspect it, but later preservation documents marked that claim as \textbf{UNCERTAIN / UNVERIFIED}.\allowbreak{}
\item This became one of the most important unresolved issues.\allowbreak{} Almost every implementation question depends on the real code: directory layout, build system, languages, existing platform/\allowbreak{}render layers, client/\allowbreak{}server dependencies, and engine/\allowbreak{}game public APIs.\allowbreak{} The next real step is repository verification.\allowbreak{}
\item INFERENCE:** The user wants future assistants to be epistemically careful: not inventing repository facts, not treating proposals as decisions, and not losing uncertainty.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture, Application Layer, TestX, and CodeHygiene Planning}

\begin{itemize}
\item The main unresolved goal is practical execution: which prompts have actually been run, which files exist, and what implementation should happen next.\allowbreak{}
\item Setup handles install, verify, repair, rollback, upgrade, downgrade, uninstall, and status.\allowbreak{} Launcher can invoke Setup, but must not replicate its mutation logic.\allowbreak{} This prevents two products from disagreeing about installation state.\allowbreak{}
\item First, verify whether \texttt{libs/\allowbreak{}contracts} and \texttt{libs/\allowbreak{}appcore} already exist.\allowbreak{} Then implement the pure contract headers and appcore skeleton.\allowbreak{} This should include:
\item Before implementing app RepoX integration, verify actual RepoX metadata paths, TestX invocation, VALIDATE-\allowbreak{}0 commands, and BUILD-\allowbreak{}ID-\allowbreak{}0 files.\allowbreak{} Without that, an implementation prompt would guess.\allowbreak{}

\item The raylib decision was also directionally accepted.\allowbreak{} The user repeatedly expressed enthusiasm for raylib and asked about using as much of raylib infrastructure as possible.\allowbreak{} The caveat is that raylib is a provider suite, not architecture.\allowbreak{} This affects rendering, audio, input, asset preview, and Workbench bootstrap.\allowbreak{}
\item 1.\allowbreak{} Verify repo baseline and dependency versions.\allowbreak{}
\item The user requires source-\allowbreak{}grounded, audit-\allowbreak{}ready, uncertainty-\allowbreak{}labelled responses.\allowbreak{} The preservation prompt explicitly requires FACT/\allowbreak{}INFERENCE/\allowbreak{}UNCERTAIN/\allowbreak{}PROJECT-\allowbreak{}CONTEXT labels, no invented facts, no treating brainstorms as decisions, and no over-\allowbreak{}compression.\allowbreak{} The prompt also requires a human-\allowbreak{}readable report first and downloadable files if possible ?filecite?turn29file0?.\allowbreak{}
\item The most blocking unresolved issues are repo baseline verification, exact dependency pins, minimal framework ABI, provider profile order, deterministic simulation spec, and license policy.\allowbreak{}
\item Verify the current \texttt{julesc013/\allowbreak{}dominium} CMake language baseline.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Language, Platform, and Architecture Baseline}

\begin{itemize}
\item The 32-\allowbreak{}bit question followed.\allowbreak{} The answer was to make full native products 64-\allowbreak{}bit and keep 32-\allowbreak{}bit as constrained or projection support only.\allowbreak{} The key caveat was not to make native pointer size part of game law.\allowbreak{} Save, replay, network, IDs, and renderer IR must use fixed-\allowbreak{}width types and never serialize size\_\allowbreak{}t, long, uintptr\_\allowbreak{}t, or raw pointers.\allowbreak{}
\item Exact tolerance for exceptions/\allowbreak{}RTTI inside private C++17 code remains unsettled.\allowbreak{} Exact platform floors and raylib/\allowbreak{}SDL2 priority remain unresolved.\allowbreak{}
\item Verify the Windows 7 and macOS 10.\allowbreak{}9 toolchain/\allowbreak{}library assumptions using official sources.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Advanced Simulation and Infrastructure Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item The user then asked whether the corridor model could be made more extensible and performant.\allowbreak{} That prompted a refinement: the editable corridor should not be the same as the runtime representation.\allowbreak{} Users and tools manipulate high-\allowbreak{}level splines, attachments, and features.\allowbreak{} The engine compiles that into microsegments, indices, occupancy bitsets, connectivity edges, structural references, and render/\allowbreak{}sim extraction data.\allowbreak{}
\item The next step was integration.\allowbreak{} The user asked what other systems this should link into.\allowbreak{} The answer identified many: RES, ENV, BUILD, TRANS, STRUCT, SIM, POWER, FLUID/\allowbreak{}HYDRO, HEAT, ATMO, VEHICLE, JOB, AGENT, logistics, inventory, rendering, save/\allowbreak{}load, replay, multiplayer checksums, ownership/\allowbreak{}authority, permissions, construction zones, and localization.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then asked whether arbitrary placement was possible or whether the system was stuck to grids.\allowbreak{} The answer established that the engine should be grid-\allowbreak{}agnostic.\allowbreak{} [FACT] The user's question directly made arbitrary placement a major topic.\allowbreak{} [INFERENCE] The answer was accepted as the current design direction because the user then asked for a Codex prompt plan to implement changes necessary to achieve it.\allowbreak{}
\item After the architecture was established, the user asked for a Codex prompt plan to implement any necessary changes.\allowbreak{} A nine-\allowbreak{}step plan was produced: coordinate/\allowbreak{}placement specs, deterministic pose/\allowbreak{}frame math, TRANS 3D corridors, STRUCT arbitrary orientation and anchors, DECOR host placement, deterministic command schemas, UI snapping refactor, performance hardening, and docs/\allowbreak{}migration notes.\allowbreak{}
\item Finally, the chat shifted into archival mode: it produced a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} Those later outputs preserved the discussion for future aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item Once the architecture was clear, the user wanted implementation planning.\allowbreak{} The chat produced a nine-\allowbreak{}step Codex prompt plan covering specs, fixed-\allowbreak{}point pose/\allowbreak{}frame math, TRANS, STRUCT, DECOR, deterministic commands, UI snapping, performance hardening, and docs.\allowbreak{}
\item Then it produced a prompt for another GPT-\allowbreak{}5.\allowbreak{}2 chat that already had a refactor/\allowbreak{}optimization plan.\allowbreak{} That prompt told the other chat to amend its existing plan instead of restarting, and to verify coverage of arbitrary placement, unified spatial primitives, co-\allowbreak{}location, signage, buildings, and determinism/\allowbreak{}performance.\allowbreak{}
\item The final phase was preservation.\allowbreak{} The user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat report reader.\allowbreak{} These artifacts were about preserving the chat for future use and aggregation, not about new architecture decisions.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium APP0 Runtime, Platform, and Renderer Architecture}

\begin{itemize}
\item The conversation started when the user provided a structured prompt called \textbf{APP0 - Runtimes, Applications, Platforms \& Renderers}.\allowbreak{} It was addressed to \textbf{GPT-5.2 Codex} and framed as part of the ongoing \textbf{Dominium / Domino project}.\allowbreak{} The prompt's purpose was to build executable and platform support correctly while staying inside the application/\allowbreak{}runtime layer.\allowbreak{}
\item The initial APP0 prompt established several hard rules.\allowbreak{} The assistant was not allowed to redesign simulation rules, alter content definitions, change life/\allowbreak{}civilization/\allowbreak{}economy logic, or introduce gameplay shortcuts.\allowbreak{} This mattered because Dominium appears to have a strong distinction between \textbf{authoritative simulation/game logic} and the shells that host, display, launch, inspect, or distribute that logic.\allowbreak{}
\item A first assistant response converted the user's APP0 prompt into a large implementation pack.\allowbreak{} It proposed repository shapes, CMake targets, executable names, renderer backends, CLI flags, smoke tests, and a commit sequence.\allowbreak{} It was useful as an implementation artifact, but it was premature relative to the user's actual desired process.\allowbreak{}
\item The user immediately corrected the direction: \textbf{"First, let's discuss this before we plan or generate any prompts."} That was an important change.\allowbreak{} It meant the user did not want a Codex prompt yet.\allowbreak{} They wanted to reason about the architecture before locking implementation instructions.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then explained that by the end of the macro-\allowbreak{}prompt plan, the project should have a \textbf{running executable} capable of displaying an \textbf{interactive and resizable window} for each supported platform and renderer combination.\allowbreak{} This made the discussion more concrete.\allowbreak{} APP0 was no longer just about docs and stubs; it had to create a real runtime path.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then stated that future implementation would have permission to modify \texttt{render}, \texttt{platform}, \texttt{application}, \texttt{client}, \texttt{server}, and docs.\allowbreak{} They asked whether this was enough or whether \texttt{engine} and \texttt{game} also needed write access.\allowbreak{}
\item The central topic was APP0: the application/\allowbreak{}runtime/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}renderer layer of Dominium.\allowbreak{} This topic came up because the user provided a formal prompt defining what APP0 should do.\allowbreak{}
\item Future work must define the client's allowed dependency surface and whether it can access any simulation-\allowbreak{}adjacent prediction layer.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Architecture, Application Layer, TestX, and CodeHygiene Planning}

\begin{itemize}

\item INFERENCE:** The user wanted to prevent future chats or Codex sessions from drifting, forgetting, or simplifying.\allowbreak{} They care deeply about maintaining continuity across many chats and turning fragmented planning into a stable project corpus.\allowbreak{}

\item The uploaded prompt requested a complete chat preservation package with human report, registers, handoff packet, spec sheet, aggregator packet, audit, and downloadable files.\allowbreak{} This output creates that package and labels its limitations.\allowbreak{}

\subsection{Dominium Canonical Architecture, Repository Foundation, and Provider Model}

\item The user wants structure to support modularity, portability, extensibility, reuse, modding, backwards compatibility, and future replacement.\allowbreak{}

\subsection{Dominium Chronology \& Celestial Systems}

\begin{itemize}
\item The final state is that this chat became both a design discussion and a packaged source document for future aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item What remains unresolved is the exact mathematical implementation of these frames and how much relativistic fidelity should exist in phase one.\allowbreak{}
\item The user asked how to extend calendars beyond planets.\allowbreak{} The answer was that the larger the scale, the less ordinary calendar structure survives.\allowbreak{} Sol can have a system-\allowbreak{}wide civil time and epoch calendar.\allowbreak{} The Milky Way should use galactic coordinate time and epoch blocks.\allowbreak{} The universe should use cosmological time and large phases/\allowbreak{}ages/\allowbreak{}eras.\allowbreak{}
\item The reason is to avoid losing context across chats and to support a future Project Spec Book.\allowbreak{} This chat produced many artifacts: prompts, reports, registers, YAML, a ZIP package, and explanatory summaries.\allowbreak{} The artifacts matter, but the substance remains the design decisions described above.\allowbreak{}
\item It is reasonable to infer that the user wants a future implementation path, likely through another assistant and eventually Codex, but does not want premature code before the broader project context is inspected.\allowbreak{}
\item It is also reasonable to infer that the user wants a future Project Spec Book assembled from multiple old-\allowbreak{}chat reports, and that this chat should contribute the chronology/\allowbreak{}celestial/\allowbreak{}timekeeping chapters.\allowbreak{}
\item This decision makes sense because it separates clean internal logic from messy external software expectations.\allowbreak{} However, it is less directly user-\allowbreak{}confirmed than the month order.\allowbreak{} Future work should ask whether compatibility projection should be default or optional.\allowbreak{}
\item The future project architecture can support data-\allowbreak{}driven systems.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Dominium Development Routes and Continuity Preservation}

\begin{itemize}
\item The conversation therefore produced both a \textbf{substantive technical proposal} and a \textbf{continuity-preservation workflow}.\allowbreak{} The technical proposal concerns how Dominium might be built.\allowbreak{} The preservation workflow concerns how the ideas from this chat should be carried forward into future chats and eventually into a larger \textbf{Project Spec Book} or \textbf{Master Living State File}.\allowbreak{}
\item Before relying on this chat as project authority, a future assistant or human reviewer must confirm whether Dominium is actually intended to be simulation-\allowbreak{}heavy, whether strict determinism matters, whether replay or multiplayer are goals, whether modding matters, and what the actual game loop is.\allowbreak{} Until then, the kernel-\allowbreak{}first plan should be treated as a strong provisional proposal, not a final specification.\allowbreak{}
\item After the initial planning answer, the user said the chat was being retired and asked for a \textbf{maximum-fidelity Context Transfer Packet}.\allowbreak{} This changed the task.\allowbreak{} The goal was no longer to continue designing Dominium directly.\allowbreak{} The goal became preserving everything necessary for a future assistant to continue without making the user repeat context.\allowbreak{}
\item The assistant produced a large Context Transfer Packet.\allowbreak{} It preserved the technical route proposal, the caveats around its uncertainty, the proposed phase order, the open questions, the risks, the artifacts, and the next actions.\allowbreak{}
\item The user then asked to turn the Context Transfer Packet and visible chat context into a final downloadable, shareable, reusable report package.\allowbreak{} This was a packaging and normalization task.\allowbreak{} The user specified exact output files: a full chat report, YAML spec sheet, aggregator packet, registers, reader brief, verification/\allowbreak{}audit file, manifest, and ZIP package.\allowbreak{}
\item After the downloadable package was generated, the user asked not to create another handoff package but to render the package contents into a readable, navigable in-\allowbreak{}chat report.\allowbreak{} This led to an "IN-\allowbreak{}CHAT REPORT READER" response.\allowbreak{} That response explained the package contents, the workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, artifacts, risks, verification queue, and next questions.\allowbreak{}
\item This topic connects to future work because choosing the route determines the order of implementation.\allowbreak{} If Route C is accepted, work begins with simulation primitives and determinism rather than UI or content.\allowbreak{}
\item Future work must decide how strict determinism really needs to be.\allowbreak{} Full determinism can be valuable, but it can also impose cost and complexity.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}
