\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}I}: The user also made a clear versioning decision.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wanted \textbf{major.minor.patch semantic versioning} for all components/\allowbreak{}packages and for the complete game package.\allowbreak{} The user explicitly did \textbf{not} want build numbers or build dates in filenames.\allowbreak{} Instead, those details should live in metadata.\allowbreak{} This became part of the future build/\allowbreak{}package design.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}II}: The last part of the chat was handoff extraction.\allowbreak{} The user asked for discovery inventory, structured registers, a context transfer packet, and then a downloadable package.\allowbreak{} The generated package captured the chat's workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, artifacts, risks, and verification items.\allowbreak{} This current report is a plain-\allowbreak{}language explanation of that substance.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}II}: The entire conversation is anchored by the requirement that Dominium must run deterministically.\allowbreak{} This means the same initial state and same inputs must produce the same state across machines, compilers, operating systems, and eventually retro and modern platforms.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}III}: The view system then expanded beyond the launcher.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wants instant zoom to any scale, instant switching between top-\allowbreak{}down 2D and first-\allowbreak{}person 3D, and arbitrary cameras for free cam, map viewing, content creation, HUD cameras, CCTV, overlays, windows, and offscreen targets.\allowbreak{} These are client/\allowbreak{}render-\allowbreak{}side concerns, not simulation concerns.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}III}: This context matters because the rest of the conversation happened inside those constraints.\allowbreak{} The launcher could not become a dumping ground for OS-\allowbreak{}specific behavior, sim mutation, renderer decisions, or nondeterministic game-\allowbreak{}state changes.\allowbreak{} It had to fit the project's deterministic layering.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: The reusable engine layer was named \textbf{Domino}.\allowbreak{} This became one of the most important naming and architectural decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} Domino is the engine/\allowbreak{}core/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}sim/\allowbreak{}mod layer, reusable for other games and projects.\allowbreak{} Dominium is the game/\allowbreak{}product layer built on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: Blueprints are plans or diffs: place element, remove element, modify terrain, place machine, connect network, etc.\allowbreak{} When accepted, they generate jobs.\allowbreak{} Manual player actions and queued work for humans, robots, or other agents should use the same job pipeline.\allowbreak{} This matters because it keeps replay, AI, and automation consistent.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Complete\_\allowbreak{}Conversation}: This chat was about preserving and re-\allowbreak{}grounding Dominium's architecture around a very old constitutional contract, then checking whether the current GitHub repository still follows that contract, and finally broadening the question into a platform-\allowbreak{}engineering doctrine for portability, modularity, extensibility, reuse, future-\allowbreak{}proofing, and long-\allowbreak{}term compatibility.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Complete\_\allowbreak{}Conversation}: The glossary bound terms such as Authority, Law, Process, Lens, SessionSpec, UniverseIdentity, Macro Capsule, SRZ, RepoX, TestX, AuditX, CompatX, SecureX, and related terms.\allowbreak{} It matters because future assistants must not use sloppy synonyms like "mode" where the canon requires ExperienceProfile or LawProfile.\allowbreak{} This topic directly supports modularity because stable vocabulary is part of stable architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: INFERENCE:** The user accepted this direction in practice, because the next request was to generate Codex prompts to implement each step fully.\allowbreak{} The user did not explicitly say, "I formally approve the Milestone-\allowbreak{}0 plan," but they proceeded to ask for prompts based on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The user then asked for recommendations.\allowbreak{} The assistant recommended a clean startup policy: explicit flags first, no environment/\allowbreak{}config override in v1, terminal detection through dsys, generic AUTO behavior, product-\allowbreak{}specific headless handling for the game, build-\allowbreak{}time GUI/\allowbreak{}TUI capabilities, and structural error codes for fallback.\allowbreak{} The user accepted this enough to request a "one big Codex prompt" to implement it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: A future assistant must understand that this chat is not simply about UI.\allowbreak{} It records the architecture for how Dominium should build itself: through contracts, commands, services, providers, documents, patches, modules, packs, apps, workspaces, diagnostics, evidence, AIDE, Codex, and eventually Workbench.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: The user then redirected the entire plan.\allowbreak{} They argued that native OS widget GUI tools already exist through Visual Studio, Xcode, etc.\allowbreak{} The project needed a cross-\allowbreak{}platform rendered tool environment that uses the same CLI, TUI, and rendered GUI systems as the client.\allowbreak{} The old UI Editor and Tool Editor were good exploratory ideas but bad final products.\allowbreak{} This produced the Dominium Workbench concept.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}setup}: Near the end, the chat shifted into preservation mode.\allowbreak{} The user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then for a downloadable report package, then for an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version of that package.\allowbreak{} Those outputs preserved the decisions, tasks, constraints, artifacts, rejected options, and verification queue for future assistants or aggregation into a larger Project Spec Book.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}setup}: These directory trees were not final, but they mattered because they exposed the user's preference for simple, shallow, logical structures and native IDE workflows that do not become the source of truth.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The original UI Editor /\allowbreak{} Tool Editor plan is now \textbf{superseded as a final product}.\allowbreak{} It is not lost: its useful parts become Workbench capabilities, especially Interface Studio, UI/\allowbreak{}HUD Sandbox, Theme Laboratory, TUI Studio, Rendered GUI Studio, Preview Matrix, validation panels, import/\allowbreak{}export tools, and document-\allowbreak{}patch workflows.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The central decision is that Workbench must not become semantic authority.\allowbreak{} Workbench is a surface over contracts, commands, services, documents, patches, providers, packs, modules, apps, artifacts, diagnostics, evidence, tests, and replay/\allowbreak{}proof.\allowbreak{} It is the richest human/\allowbreak{}agent interface over the system, but it must not bypass command, capability, refusal, validation, document, patch, or proof law.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{domino\_\allowbreak{}engine\_\allowbreak{}refactor\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: FACT:** The Domino engine must be ISO C89.\allowbreak{} The Dominium UI/\allowbreak{}tools layer must be portable C++98.\allowbreak{} Engine code must not use OS APIs.\allowbreak{} Determinism matters, so floats are disallowed in deterministic paths.\allowbreak{} The engine must avoid platform-\allowbreak{}dependent behavior and hardcoded game semantics.\allowbreak{} Everything should be content-\allowbreak{}driven.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{domino\_\allowbreak{}engine\_\allowbreak{}refactor\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: This became one of the central architectural ideas of the chat.\allowbreak{} It makes behavior extensible without giving minds direct write access to the world.\allowbreak{} A mod can add new sensors, observations, intents, capabilities, or actions, but state mutation still passes through a deterministic pipeline.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{engine\_\allowbreak{}baseline\_\allowbreak{}architecture}: This corrected the plan.\allowbreak{} The final sequencing became: first \textbf{Milestone 0: Make the baseline path honest}.\allowbreak{} That means fixing server/\allowbreak{}runtime circular import, CLI forwarding, \texttt{session\_\allowbreak{}create -\allowbreak{}> session\_\allowbreak{}boot}, missing time-\allowbreak{}anchor policy registry, and making the strict local playtest validator pass.\allowbreak{} Only after that should the builder/\allowbreak{}destruction lab begin.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{engine\_\allowbreak{}baseline\_\allowbreak{}architecture}: The final user action uploaded \texttt{Pasted text.\allowbreak{}txt}, a detailed instruction prompt requiring a full preservation report, structured registers, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, and downloadable file package for this chat.\allowbreak{} That request is the current task.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: After the root skeleton improved, the assistant and user recognized that the deeper problem was no longer top-\allowbreak{}level directories.\allowbreak{} The problem became semantic duplication and governance: what is public, what is private, what is stable, what is provisional, what is generated, what is a fixture, what must stay compatible, what can be replaced, and what proof is required.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: The key conclusion was that Workbench is not the general module system; it is one consumer of the module/\allowbreak{}command/\allowbreak{}service/\allowbreak{}provider/\allowbreak{}pack/\allowbreak{}artifact system.\allowbreak{} Workbench must not call private tools directly.\allowbreak{} It must route through registered commands and typed results, diagnostics, refusals, views, and evidence.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: Finally, the user uploaded a detailed preservation prompt and requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity report, registers, spec sheet, aggregator packet, audit, and downloadable files.\allowbreak{} This package is the response to that request.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: This became the central architectural theme.\allowbreak{} The conversation refined earlier vendor-\allowbreak{}shaped paths into service-\allowbreak{}first paths.\allowbreak{} The stable pattern is:
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: That was an important change of direction.\allowbreak{} It meant the assistant's polished prompt should not be treated as final.\allowbreak{} The work needed conceptual discussion first.\allowbreak{} The assistant then analyzed CONTENT0 and identified several issues that could cause bad assumptions to become embedded if Codex acted too soon.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: what start scenarios must explicitly exclude,
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The 32-\allowbreak{}bit question followed.\allowbreak{} The answer was to make full native products 64-\allowbreak{}bit and keep 32-\allowbreak{}bit as constrained or projection support only.\allowbreak{} The key caveat was not to make native pointer size part of game law.\allowbreak{} Save, replay, network, IDs, and renderer IR must use fixed-\allowbreak{}width types and never serialize size\_\allowbreak{}t, long, uintptr\_\allowbreak{}t, or raw pointers.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The user then consolidated a broad future plan around Domino, Dominium, Workbench, AIDE, contracts, tests, replay, and evidence.\allowbreak{} The answer agreed that the plan was strong, but identified missing central pieces: composition resolver, lockfiles, compatibility corpus, trust/\allowbreak{}permissions, virtual filesystem roots, performance budgets, and stable public-\allowbreak{}surface promotion rules.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{launcher\_\allowbreak{}app\_\allowbreak{}layer}: Someone reading this report should understand one central thing: this chat is not about inventing new launcher features anymore.\allowbreak{} It is about preserving boundaries, making the launcher implementation explicit, and ensuring future work happens on top of verified code rather than vague architectural memory.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{launcher\_\allowbreak{}app\_\allowbreak{}layer}: There was also an early suggestion to create a \texttt{DUI} facade, a Dominium UI abstraction, to support native widgets and fallback rendering.\allowbreak{} This idea was useful as a conceptual stepping stone but was not ultimately locked as a final requirement in the form originally suggested.\allowbreak{} Later application-\allowbreak{}layer canon emphasized \textbf{UI IR}, \textbf{command graphs}, and \textbf{binding validation} rather than a specific \texttt{DUI} facade design.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Launcher\_\allowbreak{}Setup\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The chat also produced multiple Codex work-\allowbreak{}order prompts and finally a downloadable report package.\allowbreak{} Those artifacts are useful, but the substance is the architecture: keep engine deterministic, keep setup/\allowbreak{}launcher/\allowbreak{}runtime boundaries explicit, make the launcher optional and modular, use a strong process/\allowbreak{}instance model, and now implement the launcher over dsys/\allowbreak{}dgfx rather than ad hoc platform UI stacks.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Launcher\_\allowbreak{}Setup\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: FACT:** The user's initial architecture text emphasized:
\item \texttt{Modularity\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Refactorability}: The final user action was uploading a preservation-\allowbreak{}package prompt.\allowbreak{} That prompt requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity preservation report, structured registers, context transfer packet, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, downloadable files, and a final in-\allowbreak{}chat reader.\allowbreak{} This package is the result.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Modularity\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Refactorability}: The user objected to the XStack/\allowbreak{}AuditX/\allowbreak{}RepoX/\allowbreak{}TestX framing and wanted AIDE adopted quickly.\allowbreak{} The resulting plan made AIDE the repo-\allowbreak{}native control plane for inventory, task queues, policies, move maps, salvage maps, evidence ledgers, validation, and refactor history.\allowbreak{} Existing tools should be recycled, not discarded.\allowbreak{} This is central to future refactorability.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{omega\_\allowbreak{}xi\_\allowbreak{}pi\_\allowbreak{}architecture\_\allowbreak{}future}: The final strategic direction before this preservation request was to run a ?-\allowbreak{}series: snapshot intake, reality extraction, blueprint reconciliation, foundation readiness, and final prompt synthesis.\allowbreak{} This is needed because plans must now be mapped onto current repo reality rather than executed abstractly.\allowbreak{} The next chat should pick up there.\allowbreak{}

\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The most important thing to remember is that this chat did not implement the refactor.\allowbreak{} It designed the architecture and generated prompts and handoff material.\allowbreak{} Any future assistant must verify actual repository state before assuming files were moved, prompts were applied, or code was updated.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: FACT:** This early discussion created the future UI/\allowbreak{}packs design context.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Control\_\allowbreak{}Plane}: The user then asked about optimizing prompts for GPT-\allowbreak{}5.\allowbreak{}3/\allowbreak{}GPT-\allowbreak{}5.\allowbreak{}4 and later supplied advice about harness engineering.\allowbreak{} That advice argued that teams succeed by engineering the environment around agents: tools, docs, linters, feedback loops, memory, and observability.\allowbreak{} The conversation accepted the core point: the model is not the whole system; the harness is the multiplier.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Control\_\allowbreak{}Plane}: The Grug Brained Developer became a design filter: avoid complexity, avoid premature abstraction, do small safe refactors, prefer integration tests, invest in logging, and respect existing structures before tearing them down.\allowbreak{} This influenced the decision to narrow AIDE and not overbuild runtime/\allowbreak{}hosts too early.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Release\_\allowbreak{}Identity\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Versioning}: The user then objected to versioning policy drift in real products and expressed concern about SemVer's "1.\allowbreak{}x forever" failure mode.\allowbreak{} The first proposed answer was a four-\allowbreak{}part public version scheme, \texttt{GEN.\allowbreak{}EPOCH.\allowbreak{}FEATURE.\allowbreak{}PATCH}, meant to avoid fake major bumps.\allowbreak{} That idea was useful as an intermediate step but later became less central because XStack already has GBN for dense build history and separate build identity.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Release\_\allowbreak{}Identity\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Versioning}: The conversation then moved into how this might fit XStack.\allowbreak{} The model shifted from "better public version number" to "layered identity model.\allowbreak{}" The assistant recommended preserving per-\allowbreak{}product versions, a global build number, build IDs, compatibility versions, and suite versions as separate layers.\allowbreak{} This became the foundation for later decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{testx\_\allowbreak{}repox\_\allowbreak{}governance}: FACT:** The user is building Dominium /\allowbreak{} Domino as a deterministic universe engine + game, not a conventional game project.\allowbreak{} The visible chat repeatedly framed the project as long-\allowbreak{}lived, simulation-\allowbreak{}first, deterministic, modular, and designed to survive across many operating systems, toolchains, renderers, products, and distribution models.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{testx\_\allowbreak{}repox\_\allowbreak{}governance}: The user then asked whether the implementation was industry-\allowbreak{}accepted and what could be improved.\allowbreak{} The response framed the approach as closer to game engines, operating systems, and long-\allowbreak{}lived infrastructure than typical game development.\allowbreak{} The important conclusion was that the design was not exotic, but it was unusually rigorous for games.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Timekeeping\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}2038\_\allowbreak{}Resilience}: A future assistant should understand that this chat contributes a time architecture doctrine for Dominium: **ACT is authority; DSYS time is runtime-\allowbreak{}only; observer clocks are derived; civil/\allowbreak{}astronomical time is projection-\allowbreak{}only; wall-\allowbreak{}clock time must never drive authoritative ordering.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Timekeeping\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}2038\_\allowbreak{}Resilience}: What remains uncertain is the precise target list of legacy machines/\allowbreak{}toolchains and how far compatibility must go, especially for 16-\allowbreak{}bit compilers with weak or absent 64-\allowbreak{}bit arithmetic.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{UE6\_\allowbreak{}Domino\_\allowbreak{}Deterministic\_\allowbreak{}Universe}: FACT: This package preserves the currently visible chat about whether Dominium should use UE6, UE5, Domino, Unreal, or a custom engine layer for a highly ambitious deterministic solar-\allowbreak{}system-\allowbreak{}scale game.\allowbreak{} It also preserves the current uploaded prompt as an artifact because it defines this preservation task.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{UE6\_\allowbreak{}Domino\_\allowbreak{}Deterministic\_\allowbreak{}Universe}: That response also introduced a rule that shaped the rest of the chat: Unreal should render and host Dominium, but Unreal should not define Dominium.\allowbreak{} That was the first major conceptual decision point.\allowbreak{} It reframed Dominium not as an engine project in the narrow sense, but as a portable game/\allowbreak{}simulation system with replaceable frontends.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The most important thing to remember is that this chat was a planning and prompt-\allowbreak{}generation chat, not proof of implementation.\allowbreak{} \textbf{UNCERTAIN / UNVERIFIED:} No generated prompt is evidence that repository code was actually changed.\allowbreak{} The next assistant must first verify whether the prompts were executed, inspect the repo/\allowbreak{}source bundle if available, and avoid treating planned files as existing files.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: This was one of the most important structure decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} It prevented the initial implementation from becoming too broad while preserving the long-\allowbreak{}term goal.\allowbreak{} The UI Editor would be a bootstrap tool, not the final architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: The pasted discussion on trees, screws, pottery, axes, chairs, tables, and machines established that the player should manipulate material, geometry, constraints, processes, tools, stations, and affordances.\allowbreak{} The conclusion is that "item classes" must not be the truth substrate.\allowbreak{} Recipes and blueprints can exist, but as higher-\allowbreak{}order formalizations.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: A major theme was that useful local inventions must become portable, standardized, industrialized, and institutionally adopted.\allowbreak{} The repo now has a Formalization Chain spec and Player Desire Acceptance Map that strongly preserve this.\allowbreak{} The future work is making this playable: drafting, measuring, testing, blueprinting, certifying, teaching, manufacturing, maintaining, and revising designs.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The user noted that earlier prompts were hard to copy because they were not always contained in one code block, and that prompts should better handle dirty worktrees and concurrent tasks.\allowbreak{} From then on, generated prompts included detailed dirty worktree handling, allowed/\allowbreak{}forbidden paths, non-\allowbreak{}goals, validation, blocker classification, and commit/\allowbreak{}final-\allowbreak{}response formats.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The final recommended sequence was: finish replay proof and barebones client, run product spine review, then begin limited parallel dev.\allowbreak{} Larger parallel waves should wait until minimum AIDE workflow law exists.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The future relevance of this chat is high.\allowbreak{} It should feed directly into the future project spec book and into a corrected implementation prompt.\allowbreak{} But it should not be treated as final implementation detail everywhere.\allowbreak{} Many high-\allowbreak{}level decisions are settled, but solver formulas, exact file encoding details, build system integration, actual repository layout, and some numerical representations still require verification.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: Before reading the rest of this report, remember this: this chat's central contribution is not one isolated feature.\allowbreak{} It is a whole design philosophy for Dominium's world engine.\allowbreak{} The game should be deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, sparse, modular, content-\allowbreak{}driven, and field-\allowbreak{}based.\allowbreak{} Chunks are not the world.\allowbreak{} Chunks are just how the world is cached, meshed, streamed, and saved.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{xstack\_\allowbreak{}lab\_\allowbreak{}galaxy}: and XStack must serve development rather than development serving XStack.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{xstack\_\allowbreak{}lab\_\allowbreak{}galaxy}: After the transcript appeared, the user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity Context Transfer Packet.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced one, with sections such as workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, risks, artifacts, and verification queue.\allowbreak{} Then the user asked to turn that into a downloadable report package.\allowbreak{} The assistant created Markdown/\allowbreak{}YAML files and a ZIP archive.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Recurring Claims}

\begin{itemize}
\item \texttt{advanced\_\allowbreak{}simulation\_\allowbreak{}infrastructure}: The user then asked whether arbitrary placement was possible or whether the system was stuck to grids.\allowbreak{} The answer established that the engine should be grid-\allowbreak{}agnostic.\allowbreak{} [FACT] The user's question directly made arbitrary placement a major topic.\allowbreak{} [INFERENCE] The answer was accepted as the current design direction because the user then asked for a Codex prompt plan to implement changes necessary to achieve it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{advanced\_\allowbreak{}simulation\_\allowbreak{}infrastructure}: Finally, the chat shifted into archival mode: it produced a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} Those later outputs preserved the discussion for future aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}ui\_\allowbreak{}providers}: The language baseline decision matters because it changes what old renderers and toolchains matter.\allowbreak{} C17/\allowbreak{}C++17 reduces the need to design around C89/\allowbreak{}C++98 constraints but does not remove the need for C-\allowbreak{}compatible ABI boundaries.\allowbreak{} The system floor decision similarly moves many older renderers and OS targets into research/\allowbreak{}back-\allowbreak{}port categories.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}ui\_\allowbreak{}providers}: The Robot OS and robotic seed-\allowbreak{}civilisation decisions are the most important design decisions.\allowbreak{} They are explicit user statements, not merely assistant proposals.\allowbreak{} They should be formalized as product/\allowbreak{}game requirements.\allowbreak{} They affect UI, fog-\allowbreak{}of-\allowbreak{}war, spawning, respawn, automation, industry, tutorial/\allowbreak{}planner design, and MMO anti-\allowbreak{}cheat.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{canonical\_\allowbreak{}structure\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}framework}: This produced a pattern: generate a large Codex/\allowbreak{}AIDE task, the user ran it or reported a result, then the assistant evaluated what was truly fixed versus what was only validator/\allowbreak{}document churn.\allowbreak{} The user eventually demanded a one-\allowbreak{}shot "actual final cleanup" prompt because previous passes had sometimes added validators without moving directories.\allowbreak{} That prompt explicitly required real \texttt{git mv} routing, not just reports.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{canonical\_\allowbreak{}structure\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}framework}: The final provider directory doctrine became service-\allowbreak{}first: \texttt{runtime/\allowbreak{}<service>/\allowbreak{}providers/\allowbreak{}<provider>/\allowbreak{}}, not \texttt{runtime/\allowbreak{}<vendor>/\allowbreak{}<service>/\allowbreak{}}.\allowbreak{} Provider choices belong in \texttt{release/\allowbreak{}profiles/\allowbreak{}} or \texttt{content/\allowbreak{}profiles/\allowbreak{}}, not app path names.\allowbreak{} External code belongs under \texttt{external/\allowbreak{}upstream/\allowbreak{}} or \texttt{external/\allowbreak{}vendor/\allowbreak{}}, but the repo should choose one convention.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{development\_\allowbreak{}routes}: Before relying on this chat as project authority, a future assistant or human reviewer must confirm whether Dominium is actually intended to be simulation-\allowbreak{}heavy, whether strict determinism matters, whether replay or multiplayer are goals, whether modding matters, and what the actual game loop is.\allowbreak{} Until then, the kernel-\allowbreak{}first plan should be treated as a strong provisional proposal, not a final specification.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{development\_\allowbreak{}routes}: The first answer was assertive.\allowbreak{} It stated that Dominium must follow Route C and described the route as the only viable one for Dominium.\allowbreak{} Later parts of the chat corrected the status of that claim.\allowbreak{} FACT: the assistant made that recommendation.\allowbreak{} UNCERTAIN /\allowbreak{} UNVERIFIED: the recommendation was not validated with external sources in the chat and was not explicitly accepted by the user.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: The reusable engine layer was named \textbf{Domino}.\allowbreak{} This became one of the most important naming and architectural decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} Domino is the engine/\allowbreak{}core/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}sim/\allowbreak{}mod layer, reusable for other games and projects.\allowbreak{} Dominium is the game/\allowbreak{}product layer built on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: Blueprints are plans or diffs: place element, remove element, modify terrain, place machine, connect network, etc.\allowbreak{} When accepted, they generate jobs.\allowbreak{} Manual player actions and queued work for humans, robots, or other agents should use the same job pipeline.\allowbreak{} This matters because it keeps replay, AI, and automation consistent.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: INFERENCE:** The user accepted this direction in practice, because the next request was to generate Codex prompts to implement each step fully.\allowbreak{} The user did not explicitly say, "I formally approve the Milestone-\allowbreak{}0 plan," but they proceeded to ask for prompts based on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The user then asked for recommendations.\allowbreak{} The assistant recommended a clean startup policy: explicit flags first, no environment/\allowbreak{}config override in v1, terminal detection through dsys, generic AUTO behavior, product-\allowbreak{}specific headless handling for the game, build-\allowbreak{}time GUI/\allowbreak{}TUI capabilities, and structural error codes for fallback.\allowbreak{} The user accepted this enough to request a "one big Codex prompt" to implement it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: A future assistant must understand that this chat is not simply about UI.\allowbreak{} It records the architecture for how Dominium should build itself: through contracts, commands, services, providers, documents, patches, modules, packs, apps, workspaces, diagnostics, evidence, AIDE, Codex, and eventually Workbench.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: The user then redirected the entire plan.\allowbreak{} They argued that native OS widget GUI tools already exist through Visual Studio, Xcode, etc.\allowbreak{} The project needed a cross-\allowbreak{}platform rendered tool environment that uses the same CLI, TUI, and rendered GUI systems as the client.\allowbreak{} The old UI Editor and Tool Editor were good exploratory ideas but bad final products.\allowbreak{} This produced the Dominium Workbench concept.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The original UI Editor /\allowbreak{} Tool Editor plan is now \textbf{superseded as a final product}.\allowbreak{} It is not lost: its useful parts become Workbench capabilities, especially Interface Studio, UI/\allowbreak{}HUD Sandbox, Theme Laboratory, TUI Studio, Rendered GUI Studio, Preview Matrix, validation panels, import/\allowbreak{}export tools, and document-\allowbreak{}patch workflows.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The central decision is that Workbench must not become semantic authority.\allowbreak{} Workbench is a surface over contracts, commands, services, documents, patches, providers, packs, modules, apps, artifacts, diagnostics, evidence, tests, and replay/\allowbreak{}proof.\allowbreak{} It is the richest human/\allowbreak{}agent interface over the system, but it must not bypass command, capability, refusal, validation, document, patch, or proof law.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{engine\_\allowbreak{}baseline\_\allowbreak{}architecture}: This corrected the plan.\allowbreak{} The final sequencing became: first \textbf{Milestone 0: Make the baseline path honest}.\allowbreak{} That means fixing server/\allowbreak{}runtime circular import, CLI forwarding, \texttt{session\_\allowbreak{}create -\allowbreak{}> session\_\allowbreak{}boot}, missing time-\allowbreak{}anchor policy registry, and making the strict local playtest validator pass.\allowbreak{} Only after that should the builder/\allowbreak{}destruction lab begin.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{engine\_\allowbreak{}baseline\_\allowbreak{}architecture}: The final user action uploaded \texttt{Pasted text.\allowbreak{}txt}, a detailed instruction prompt requiring a full preservation report, structured registers, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, and downloadable file package for this chat.\allowbreak{} That request is the current task.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: After the root skeleton improved, the assistant and user recognized that the deeper problem was no longer top-\allowbreak{}level directories.\allowbreak{} The problem became semantic duplication and governance: what is public, what is private, what is stable, what is provisional, what is generated, what is a fixture, what must stay compatible, what can be replaced, and what proof is required.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: The key conclusion was that Workbench is not the general module system; it is one consumer of the module/\allowbreak{}command/\allowbreak{}service/\allowbreak{}provider/\allowbreak{}pack/\allowbreak{}artifact system.\allowbreak{} Workbench must not call private tools directly.\allowbreak{} It must route through registered commands and typed results, diagnostics, refusals, views, and evidence.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: Finally, the user uploaded a detailed preservation prompt and requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity report, registers, spec sheet, aggregator packet, audit, and downloadable files.\allowbreak{} This package is the response to that request.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: This became the central architectural theme.\allowbreak{} The conversation refined earlier vendor-\allowbreak{}shaped paths into service-\allowbreak{}first paths.\allowbreak{} The stable pattern is:
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: That was an important change of direction.\allowbreak{} It meant the assistant's polished prompt should not be treated as final.\allowbreak{} The work needed conceptual discussion first.\allowbreak{} The assistant then analyzed CONTENT0 and identified several issues that could cause bad assumptions to become embedded if Codex acted too soon.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: what start scenarios must explicitly exclude,
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The 32-\allowbreak{}bit question followed.\allowbreak{} The answer was to make full native products 64-\allowbreak{}bit and keep 32-\allowbreak{}bit as constrained or projection support only.\allowbreak{} The key caveat was not to make native pointer size part of game law.\allowbreak{} Save, replay, network, IDs, and renderer IR must use fixed-\allowbreak{}width types and never serialize size\_\allowbreak{}t, long, uintptr\_\allowbreak{}t, or raw pointers.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The user then consolidated a broad future plan around Domino, Dominium, Workbench, AIDE, contracts, tests, replay, and evidence.\allowbreak{} The answer agreed that the plan was strong, but identified missing central pieces: composition resolver, lockfiles, compatibility corpus, trust/\allowbreak{}permissions, virtual filesystem roots, performance budgets, and stable public-\allowbreak{}surface promotion rules.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{OS\_\allowbreak{}Interface\_\allowbreak{}Repo\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: DECISION-\allowbreak{}01 -\allowbreak{} CLI mandatory, TUI expected, GUI modular.\allowbreak{}** This was part of the initial architecture baseline and was repeatedly reaffirmed.\allowbreak{} It matters because every product must remain operable in recovery/\allowbreak{}headless/\allowbreak{}automation contexts.\allowbreak{} GUI is allowed but not authoritative.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{OS\_\allowbreak{}Interface\_\allowbreak{}Repo\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: DECISION-\allowbreak{}02 -\allowbreak{} Thin GUI shells over shared contracts.\allowbreak{}** The chat consistently rejected GUI families becoming separate product architectures.\allowbreak{} This affects client, launcher, setup, server admin, tools, and Workbench modules.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}renderer\_\allowbreak{}api\_\allowbreak{}plan}: The chat ended by generating a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} The main thing to remember is this: **the active artifact is the final 9-\allowbreak{}prompt post-\allowbreak{}master Codex plan, but it is a plan, not evidence that the code exists.\allowbreak{} The repo must be inspected before execution.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}renderer\_\allowbreak{}api\_\allowbreak{}plan}: These ideas became the architectural heart of the final plan.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The most important thing to remember is that this chat did not implement the refactor.\allowbreak{} It designed the architecture and generated prompts and handoff material.\allowbreak{} Any future assistant must verify actual repository state before assuming files were moved, prompts were applied, or code was updated.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: FACT:** This early discussion created the future UI/\allowbreak{}packs design context.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The most important thing to remember is that this chat was a planning and prompt-\allowbreak{}generation chat, not proof of implementation.\allowbreak{} \textbf{UNCERTAIN / UNVERIFIED:} No generated prompt is evidence that repository code was actually changed.\allowbreak{} The next assistant must first verify whether the prompts were executed, inspect the repo/\allowbreak{}source bundle if available, and avoid treating planned files as existing files.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: This was one of the most important structure decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} It prevented the initial implementation from becoming too broad while preserving the long-\allowbreak{}term goal.\allowbreak{} The UI Editor would be a bootstrap tool, not the final architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: The pasted discussion on trees, screws, pottery, axes, chairs, tables, and machines established that the player should manipulate material, geometry, constraints, processes, tools, stations, and affordances.\allowbreak{} The conclusion is that "item classes" must not be the truth substrate.\allowbreak{} Recipes and blueprints can exist, but as higher-\allowbreak{}order formalizations.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: A major theme was that useful local inventions must become portable, standardized, industrialized, and institutionally adopted.\allowbreak{} The repo now has a Formalization Chain spec and Player Desire Acceptance Map that strongly preserve this.\allowbreak{} The future work is making this playable: drafting, measuring, testing, blueprinting, certifying, teaching, manufacturing, maintaining, and revising designs.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The user noted that earlier prompts were hard to copy because they were not always contained in one code block, and that prompts should better handle dirty worktrees and concurrent tasks.\allowbreak{} From then on, generated prompts included detailed dirty worktree handling, allowed/\allowbreak{}forbidden paths, non-\allowbreak{}goals, validation, blocker classification, and commit/\allowbreak{}final-\allowbreak{}response formats.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The final recommended sequence was: finish replay proof and barebones client, run product spine review, then begin limited parallel dev.\allowbreak{} Larger parallel waves should wait until minimum AIDE workflow law exists.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The future relevance of this chat is high.\allowbreak{} It should feed directly into the future project spec book and into a corrected implementation prompt.\allowbreak{} But it should not be treated as final implementation detail everywhere.\allowbreak{} Many high-\allowbreak{}level decisions are settled, but solver formulas, exact file encoding details, build system integration, actual repository layout, and some numerical representations still require verification.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: Before reading the rest of this report, remember this: this chat's central contribution is not one isolated feature.\allowbreak{} It is a whole design philosophy for Dominium's world engine.\allowbreak{} The game should be deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, sparse, modular, content-\allowbreak{}driven, and field-\allowbreak{}based.\allowbreak{} Chunks are not the world.\allowbreak{} Chunks are just how the world is cached, meshed, streamed, and saved.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}

\section{Topic: worldgen}

\subsection{What This Topic Means}

\texttt{worldgen} is an automatically classified conversation-\allowbreak{}corpus topic.\allowbreak{} It groups archived conversations by recurring words and package labels, not by authoritative repo ownership.\allowbreak{}

\subsection{Recurring Claims}

\begin{itemize}
\item \texttt{app\_\allowbreak{}runtime\_\allowbreak{}platform\_\allowbreak{}renderers}: The central topic was APP0: the application/\allowbreak{}runtime/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}renderer layer of Dominium.\allowbreak{} This topic came up because the user provided a formal prompt defining what APP0 should do.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{app\_\allowbreak{}runtime\_\allowbreak{}platform\_\allowbreak{}renderers}: The most important conclusion was that APP0 must remain outside the core simulation rules.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} the user explicitly forbade redesigning simulation rules, altering content definitions, changing life/\allowbreak{}civilization/\allowbreak{}economy logic, and introducing gameplay shortcuts.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} authoritative logic remains in engine + game.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{app\_\allowbreak{}testx\_\allowbreak{}codehygiene}: A large set of prompts was then generated to lock down architecture, determinism, performance, schema governance, rendering, epistemic UI, sharding, interest sets, and fidelity projection.\allowbreak{} This became the Phase 1 hardening layer.\allowbreak{} Additional audit prompts were introduced to ensure consistency before proceeding into life, civilization, war, content, agents, tools, mods, and final long-\allowbreak{}term policy.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{app\_\allowbreak{}testx\_\allowbreak{}codehygiene}: Because the chat was huge, the user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet.\allowbreak{} Then they asked for downloadable report files.\allowbreak{} Then they asked for an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader.\allowbreak{} Finally, they asked for this human-\allowbreak{}readable explanatory report.\allowbreak{} This final report is meant to let a future assistant or human understand the substance without re-\allowbreak{}reading the whole conversation.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: The most important thing to remember is this: \textbf{this chat is the architectural and implementation-roadmap backbone for Dominium/Domino, but it is not an implementation log}.\allowbreak{} It should be preserved as a design/\allowbreak{}specification source and as a prompt library, while actual code and current facts must be verified separately.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: FACT: The user opened by pasting an "Extended Master Starter Prompt -\allowbreak{} Dominium + Domino.\allowbreak{}" That prompt established the role of the assistant as a senior engine architect and defined the main project philosophy.\allowbreak{} Domino was described as a deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, C89 engine portable across old and modern OS/\allowbreak{}architecture combinations.\allowbreak{} Dominium was described as the product suite built on top of Domino.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}ui\_\allowbreak{}providers}: The language baseline decision matters because it changes what old renderers and toolchains matter.\allowbreak{} C17/\allowbreak{}C++17 reduces the need to design around C89/\allowbreak{}C++98 constraints but does not remove the need for C-\allowbreak{}compatible ABI boundaries.\allowbreak{} The system floor decision similarly moves many older renderers and OS targets into research/\allowbreak{}back-\allowbreak{}port categories.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{architecture\_\allowbreak{}ui\_\allowbreak{}providers}: The Robot OS and robotic seed-\allowbreak{}civilisation decisions are the most important design decisions.\allowbreak{} They are explicit user statements, not merely assistant proposals.\allowbreak{} They should be formalized as product/\allowbreak{}game requirements.\allowbreak{} They affect UI, fog-\allowbreak{}of-\allowbreak{}war, spawning, respawn, automation, industry, tutorial/\allowbreak{}planner design, and MMO anti-\allowbreak{}cheat.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Build\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Future\_\allowbreak{}Proofing}: Plain-\allowbreak{}language limitation: this package is reliable as a preservation of the current visible chat.\allowbreak{} It should not be treated as a complete archive of every Dominium discussion in other chats.\allowbreak{} Where the report uses project or repository context, it is labelled as such.\allowbreak{} The most important caveat is that many items are recommendations, not final user decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Build\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Future\_\allowbreak{}Proofing}: The final uploaded prompt requested a complete preservation package for this chat: a human-\allowbreak{}readable explanation, structured registers, context transfer packet, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, and files/\allowbreak{}ZIP package.\allowbreak{} This response completes that export task and creates downloadable files for later reading and aggregation.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Chronology\_\allowbreak{}Celestial\_\allowbreak{}Systems}: This reinforced the core celestial-\allowbreak{}content rule: locations should exist explicitly when they change player decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Chronology\_\allowbreak{}Celestial\_\allowbreak{}Systems}: This became the spatial foundation for later time/\allowbreak{}calendar decisions because every major body or region could potentially have its own local time, calendar, or operational standard.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{development\_\allowbreak{}routes}: Before relying on this chat as project authority, a future assistant or human reviewer must confirm whether Dominium is actually intended to be simulation-\allowbreak{}heavy, whether strict determinism matters, whether replay or multiplayer are goals, whether modding matters, and what the actual game loop is.\allowbreak{} Until then, the kernel-\allowbreak{}first plan should be treated as a strong provisional proposal, not a final specification.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{development\_\allowbreak{}routes}: The first answer was assertive.\allowbreak{} It stated that Dominium must follow Route C and described the route as the only viable one for Dominium.\allowbreak{} Later parts of the chat corrected the status of that claim.\allowbreak{} FACT: the assistant made that recommendation.\allowbreak{} UNCERTAIN /\allowbreak{} UNVERIFIED: the recommendation was not validated with external sources in the chat and was not explicitly accepted by the user.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}I}: During this stage, the work was still broadly about "the project spec.\allowbreak{}" The user wanted a comprehensive description of the game and its technical systems.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced large design documents, but many of those earlier contents are not visible in the retained transcript.\allowbreak{} This is important because the final report package marks those earlier outputs as referenced but not fully recovered.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}I}: The user also made a clear versioning decision.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wanted \textbf{major.minor.patch semantic versioning} for all components/\allowbreak{}packages and for the complete game package.\allowbreak{} The user explicitly did \textbf{not} want build numbers or build dates in filenames.\allowbreak{} Instead, those details should live in metadata.\allowbreak{} This became part of the future build/\allowbreak{}package design.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}II}: The last part of the chat was handoff extraction.\allowbreak{} The user asked for discovery inventory, structured registers, a context transfer packet, and then a downloadable package.\allowbreak{} The generated package captured the chat's workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, artifacts, risks, and verification items.\allowbreak{} This current report is a plain-\allowbreak{}language explanation of that substance.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}II}: The entire conversation is anchored by the requirement that Dominium must run deterministically.\allowbreak{} This means the same initial state and same inputs must produce the same state across machines, compilers, operating systems, and eventually retro and modern platforms.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}III}: The view system then expanded beyond the launcher.\allowbreak{} \textbf{FACT:} The user wants instant zoom to any scale, instant switching between top-\allowbreak{}down 2D and first-\allowbreak{}person 3D, and arbitrary cameras for free cam, map viewing, content creation, HUD cameras, CCTV, overlays, windows, and offscreen targets.\allowbreak{} These are client/\allowbreak{}render-\allowbreak{}side concerns, not simulation concerns.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}III}: This context matters because the rest of the conversation happened inside those constraints.\allowbreak{} The launcher could not become a dumping ground for OS-\allowbreak{}specific behavior, sim mutation, renderer decisions, or nondeterministic game-\allowbreak{}state changes.\allowbreak{} It had to fit the project's deterministic layering.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: The reusable engine layer was named \textbf{Domino}.\allowbreak{} This became one of the most important naming and architectural decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} Domino is the engine/\allowbreak{}core/\allowbreak{}platform/\allowbreak{}sim/\allowbreak{}mod layer, reusable for other games and projects.\allowbreak{} Dominium is the game/\allowbreak{}product layer built on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Architecture\_\allowbreak{}IV}: Blueprints are plans or diffs: place element, remove element, modify terrain, place machine, connect network, etc.\allowbreak{} When accepted, they generate jobs.\allowbreak{} Manual player actions and queued work for humans, robots, or other agents should use the same job pipeline.\allowbreak{} This matters because it keeps replay, AI, and automation consistent.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Complete\_\allowbreak{}Conversation}: This chat was about preserving and re-\allowbreak{}grounding Dominium's architecture around a very old constitutional contract, then checking whether the current GitHub repository still follows that contract, and finally broadening the question into a platform-\allowbreak{}engineering doctrine for portability, modularity, extensibility, reuse, future-\allowbreak{}proofing, and long-\allowbreak{}term compatibility.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Complete\_\allowbreak{}Conversation}: The glossary bound terms such as Authority, Law, Process, Lens, SessionSpec, UniverseIdentity, Macro Capsule, SRZ, RepoX, TestX, AuditX, CompatX, SecureX, and related terms.\allowbreak{} It matters because future assistants must not use sloppy synonyms like "mode" where the canon requires ExperienceProfile or LawProfile.\allowbreak{} This topic directly supports modularity because stable vocabulary is part of stable architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: INFERENCE:** The user accepted this direction in practice, because the next request was to generate Codex prompts to implement each step fully.\allowbreak{} The user did not explicitly say, "I formally approve the Milestone-\allowbreak{}0 plan," but they proceeded to ask for prompts based on it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}domino\_\allowbreak{}codex\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The user then asked for recommendations.\allowbreak{} The assistant recommended a clean startup policy: explicit flags first, no environment/\allowbreak{}config override in v1, terminal detection through dsys, generic AUTO behavior, product-\allowbreak{}specific headless handling for the game, build-\allowbreak{}time GUI/\allowbreak{}TUI capabilities, and structural error codes for fallback.\allowbreak{} The user accepted this enough to request a "one big Codex prompt" to implement it.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: A future assistant must understand that this chat is not simply about UI.\allowbreak{} It records the architecture for how Dominium should build itself: through contracts, commands, services, providers, documents, patches, modules, packs, apps, workspaces, diagnostics, evidence, AIDE, Codex, and eventually Workbench.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}full\_\allowbreak{}conversation}: The user then redirected the entire plan.\allowbreak{} They argued that native OS widget GUI tools already exist through Visual Studio, Xcode, etc.\allowbreak{} The project needed a cross-\allowbreak{}platform rendered tool environment that uses the same CLI, TUI, and rendered GUI systems as the client.\allowbreak{} The old UI Editor and Tool Editor were good exploratory ideas but bad final products.\allowbreak{} This produced the Dominium Workbench concept.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}setup}: Near the end, the chat shifted into preservation mode.\allowbreak{} The user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet, then for a downloadable report package, then for an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version of that package.\allowbreak{} Those outputs preserved the decisions, tasks, constraints, artifacts, rejected options, and verification queue for future assistants or aggregation into a larger Project Spec Book.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{dominium\_\allowbreak{}setup}: These directory trees were not final, but they mattered because they exposed the user's preference for simple, shallow, logical structures and native IDE workflows that do not become the source of truth.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The original UI Editor /\allowbreak{} Tool Editor plan is now \textbf{superseded as a final product}.\allowbreak{} It is not lost: its useful parts become Workbench capabilities, especially Interface Studio, UI/\allowbreak{}HUD Sandbox, Theme Laboratory, TUI Studio, Rendered GUI Studio, Preview Matrix, validation panels, import/\allowbreak{}export tools, and document-\allowbreak{}patch workflows.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Domino\_\allowbreak{}Dominium\_\allowbreak{}Workbench}: The central decision is that Workbench must not become semantic authority.\allowbreak{} Workbench is a surface over contracts, commands, services, documents, patches, providers, packs, modules, apps, artifacts, diagnostics, evidence, tests, and replay/\allowbreak{}proof.\allowbreak{} It is the richest human/\allowbreak{}agent interface over the system, but it must not bypass command, capability, refusal, validation, document, patch, or proof law.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{domino\_\allowbreak{}engine\_\allowbreak{}refactor\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: FACT:** The Domino engine must be ISO C89.\allowbreak{} The Dominium UI/\allowbreak{}tools layer must be portable C++98.\allowbreak{} Engine code must not use OS APIs.\allowbreak{} Determinism matters, so floats are disallowed in deterministic paths.\allowbreak{} The engine must avoid platform-\allowbreak{}dependent behavior and hardcoded game semantics.\allowbreak{} Everything should be content-\allowbreak{}driven.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{domino\_\allowbreak{}engine\_\allowbreak{}refactor\_\allowbreak{}prompts}: This became one of the central architectural ideas of the chat.\allowbreak{} It makes behavior extensible without giving minds direct write access to the world.\allowbreak{} A mod can add new sensors, observations, intents, capabilities, or actions, but state mutation still passes through a deterministic pipeline.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: After the root skeleton improved, the assistant and user recognized that the deeper problem was no longer top-\allowbreak{}level directories.\allowbreak{} The problem became semantic duplication and governance: what is public, what is private, what is stable, what is provisional, what is generated, what is a fixture, what must stay compatible, what can be replaced, and what proof is required.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Foundation\_\allowbreak{}Workbench\_\allowbreak{}Codex}: The key conclusion was that Workbench is not the general module system; it is one consumer of the module/\allowbreak{}command/\allowbreak{}service/\allowbreak{}provider/\allowbreak{}pack/\allowbreak{}artifact system.\allowbreak{} Workbench must not call private tools directly.\allowbreak{} It must route through registered commands and typed results, diagnostics, refusals, views, and evidence.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: Finally, the user uploaded a detailed preservation prompt and requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity report, registers, spec sheet, aggregator packet, audit, and downloadable files.\allowbreak{} This package is the response to that request.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Framework\_\allowbreak{}Open\_\allowbreak{}Source\_\allowbreak{}Provider}: This became the central architectural theme.\allowbreak{} The conversation refined earlier vendor-\allowbreak{}shaped paths into service-\allowbreak{}first paths.\allowbreak{} The stable pattern is:
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: That was an important change of direction.\allowbreak{} It meant the assistant's polished prompt should not be treated as final.\allowbreak{} The work needed conceptual discussion first.\allowbreak{} The assistant then analyzed CONTENT0 and identified several issues that could cause bad assumptions to become embedded if Codex acted too soon.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{gui\_\allowbreak{}binary\_\allowbreak{}content}: what start scenarios must explicitly exclude,
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The 32-\allowbreak{}bit question followed.\allowbreak{} The answer was to make full native products 64-\allowbreak{}bit and keep 32-\allowbreak{}bit as constrained or projection support only.\allowbreak{} The key caveat was not to make native pointer size part of game law.\allowbreak{} Save, replay, network, IDs, and renderer IR must use fixed-\allowbreak{}width types and never serialize size\_\allowbreak{}t, long, uintptr\_\allowbreak{}t, or raw pointers.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Language\_\allowbreak{}Platform\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The user then consolidated a broad future plan around Domino, Dominium, Workbench, AIDE, contracts, tests, replay, and evidence.\allowbreak{} The answer agreed that the plan was strong, but identified missing central pieces: composition resolver, lockfiles, compatibility corpus, trust/\allowbreak{}permissions, virtual filesystem roots, performance budgets, and stable public-\allowbreak{}surface promotion rules.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{launcher\_\allowbreak{}app\_\allowbreak{}layer}: Someone reading this report should understand one central thing: this chat is not about inventing new launcher features anymore.\allowbreak{} It is about preserving boundaries, making the launcher implementation explicit, and ensuring future work happens on top of verified code rather than vague architectural memory.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{launcher\_\allowbreak{}app\_\allowbreak{}layer}: There was also an early suggestion to create a \texttt{DUI} facade, a Dominium UI abstraction, to support native widgets and fallback rendering.\allowbreak{} This idea was useful as a conceptual stepping stone but was not ultimately locked as a final requirement in the form originally suggested.\allowbreak{} Later application-\allowbreak{}layer canon emphasized \textbf{UI IR}, \textbf{command graphs}, and \textbf{binding validation} rather than a specific \texttt{DUI} facade design.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Launcher\_\allowbreak{}Setup\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The chat also produced multiple Codex work-\allowbreak{}order prompts and finally a downloadable report package.\allowbreak{} Those artifacts are useful, but the substance is the architecture: keep engine deterministic, keep setup/\allowbreak{}launcher/\allowbreak{}runtime boundaries explicit, make the launcher optional and modular, use a strong process/\allowbreak{}instance model, and now implement the launcher over dsys/\allowbreak{}dgfx rather than ad hoc platform UI stacks.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Launcher\_\allowbreak{}Setup\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: FACT:** The user's initial architecture text emphasized:
\item \texttt{Modularity\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Refactorability}: The final user action was uploading a preservation-\allowbreak{}package prompt.\allowbreak{} That prompt requested a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity preservation report, structured registers, context transfer packet, spec sheet, aggregator packet, self-\allowbreak{}audit, downloadable files, and a final in-\allowbreak{}chat reader.\allowbreak{} This package is the result.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Modularity\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Refactorability}: The user objected to the XStack/\allowbreak{}AuditX/\allowbreak{}RepoX/\allowbreak{}TestX framing and wanted AIDE adopted quickly.\allowbreak{} The resulting plan made AIDE the repo-\allowbreak{}native control plane for inventory, task queues, policies, move maps, salvage maps, evidence ledgers, validation, and refactor history.\allowbreak{} Existing tools should be recycled, not discarded.\allowbreak{} This is central to future refactorability.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{omega\_\allowbreak{}xi\_\allowbreak{}pi\_\allowbreak{}architecture\_\allowbreak{}future}: The final strategic direction before this preservation request was to run a ?-\allowbreak{}series: snapshot intake, reality extraction, blueprint reconciliation, foundation readiness, and final prompt synthesis.\allowbreak{} This is needed because plans must now be mapped onto current repo reality rather than executed abstractly.\allowbreak{} The next chat should pick up there.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{omega\_\allowbreak{}xi\_\allowbreak{}pi\_\allowbreak{}architecture\_\allowbreak{}future}: The user later reported Xi completion.\allowbreak{} The enduring lesson is that prompt instructions are not enough; architecture must be machine-\allowbreak{}readable and enforced.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}renderer\_\allowbreak{}api\_\allowbreak{}plan}: The chat ended by generating a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity transfer packet, then a downloadable report package, then an in-\allowbreak{}chat reader version.\allowbreak{} The main thing to remember is this: **the active artifact is the final 9-\allowbreak{}prompt post-\allowbreak{}master Codex plan, but it is a plan, not evidence that the code exists.\allowbreak{} The repo must be inspected before execution.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}renderer\_\allowbreak{}api\_\allowbreak{}plan}: These ideas became the architectural heart of the final plan.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}support}: FACT: That early answer was generated by the assistant, not stated by the user as a final decision.\allowbreak{} It matters because it introduced a very broad portability ambition, but it was later superseded by more practical platform planning.\allowbreak{} It should now be treated as historical context or possible research scope, not as a controlling product commitment.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{platform\_\allowbreak{}support}: The user then supplied a detailed inventory covering PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo, PC handhelds, Android devices, Apple devices, Web platforms, AR, VR, and cross-\allowbreak{}cutting software targets.\allowbreak{} This inventory became the central artifact of the chat.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Portability\_\allowbreak{}Assurance\_\allowbreak{}Future\_\allowbreak{}Proof}: The actual repo was not inspected.\allowbreak{} The exact accepted directory structure, API policy, DDAP profile, compatibility promises, and first pilot module remain unresolved.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Portability\_\allowbreak{}Assurance\_\allowbreak{}Future\_\allowbreak{}Proof}: Status: Assistant recommendation; not separately accepted after answer.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{readme\_\allowbreak{}ports\_\allowbreak{}determinism}: The user pasted the final README after those cleanup changes.\allowbreak{} At that point, the README had the current active form: deterministic constraints clarified, ports unified under one source hierarchy, \texttt{/\allowbreak{}ports} metadata-\allowbreak{}only if retained, Section 9 normative, lockstep canonical, content-\allowbreak{}lock mismatch fatal, and disk format versions immutable.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{readme\_\allowbreak{}ports\_\allowbreak{}determinism}: After the README work, the user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity context transfer packet.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced a long state-\allowbreak{}transfer document with sections for project inventory, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, artifacts, risks, and next actions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Control\_\allowbreak{}Plane}: The user then asked about optimizing prompts for GPT-\allowbreak{}5.\allowbreak{}3/\allowbreak{}GPT-\allowbreak{}5.\allowbreak{}4 and later supplied advice about harness engineering.\allowbreak{} That advice argued that teams succeed by engineering the environment around agents: tools, docs, linters, feedback loops, memory, and observability.\allowbreak{} The conversation accepted the core point: the model is not the whole system; the harness is the multiplier.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Refactor\_\allowbreak{}Control\_\allowbreak{}Plane}: The Grug Brained Developer became a design filter: avoid complexity, avoid premature abstraction, do small safe refactors, prefer integration tests, invest in logging, and respect existing structures before tearing them down.\allowbreak{} This influenced the decision to narrow AIDE and not overbuild runtime/\allowbreak{}hosts too early.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Release\_\allowbreak{}Identity\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Versioning}: The user then objected to versioning policy drift in real products and expressed concern about SemVer's "1.\allowbreak{}x forever" failure mode.\allowbreak{} The first proposed answer was a four-\allowbreak{}part public version scheme, \texttt{GEN.\allowbreak{}EPOCH.\allowbreak{}FEATURE.\allowbreak{}PATCH}, meant to avoid fake major bumps.\allowbreak{} That idea was useful as an intermediate step but later became less central because XStack already has GBN for dense build history and separate build identity.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Release\_\allowbreak{}Identity\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}Versioning}: The conversation then moved into how this might fit XStack.\allowbreak{} The model shifted from "better public version number" to "layered identity model.\allowbreak{}" The assistant recommended preserving per-\allowbreak{}product versions, a global build number, build IDs, compatibility versions, and suite versions as separate layers.\allowbreak{} This became the foundation for later decisions.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{testx\_\allowbreak{}repox\_\allowbreak{}governance}: FACT:** The user is building Dominium /\allowbreak{} Domino as a deterministic universe engine + game, not a conventional game project.\allowbreak{} The visible chat repeatedly framed the project as long-\allowbreak{}lived, simulation-\allowbreak{}first, deterministic, modular, and designed to survive across many operating systems, toolchains, renderers, products, and distribution models.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{testx\_\allowbreak{}repox\_\allowbreak{}governance}: The user then asked whether the implementation was industry-\allowbreak{}accepted and what could be improved.\allowbreak{} The response framed the approach as closer to game engines, operating systems, and long-\allowbreak{}lived infrastructure than typical game development.\allowbreak{} The important conclusion was that the design was not exotic, but it was unusually rigorous for games.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Timekeeping\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}2038\_\allowbreak{}Resilience}: A future assistant should understand that this chat contributes a time architecture doctrine for Dominium: **ACT is authority; DSYS time is runtime-\allowbreak{}only; observer clocks are derived; civil/\allowbreak{}astronomical time is projection-\allowbreak{}only; wall-\allowbreak{}clock time must never drive authoritative ordering.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Timekeeping\_\allowbreak{}and\_\allowbreak{}2038\_\allowbreak{}Resilience}: What remains uncertain is the precise target list of legacy machines/\allowbreak{}toolchains and how far compatibility must go, especially for 16-\allowbreak{}bit compilers with weak or absent 64-\allowbreak{}bit arithmetic.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{UE6\_\allowbreak{}Domino\_\allowbreak{}Deterministic\_\allowbreak{}Universe}: FACT: This package preserves the currently visible chat about whether Dominium should use UE6, UE5, Domino, Unreal, or a custom engine layer for a highly ambitious deterministic solar-\allowbreak{}system-\allowbreak{}scale game.\allowbreak{} It also preserves the current uploaded prompt as an artifact because it defines this preservation task.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{UE6\_\allowbreak{}Domino\_\allowbreak{}Deterministic\_\allowbreak{}Universe}: That response also introduced a rule that shaped the rest of the chat: Unreal should render and host Dominium, but Unreal should not define Dominium.\allowbreak{} That was the first major conceptual decision point.\allowbreak{} It reframed Dominium not as an engine project in the narrow sense, but as a portable game/\allowbreak{}simulation system with replaceable frontends.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: The most important thing to remember is that this chat was a planning and prompt-\allowbreak{}generation chat, not proof of implementation.\allowbreak{} \textbf{UNCERTAIN / UNVERIFIED:} No generated prompt is evidence that repository code was actually changed.\allowbreak{} The next assistant must first verify whether the prompts were executed, inspect the repo/\allowbreak{}source bundle if available, and avoid treating planned files as existing files.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{ui\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}tool\_\allowbreak{}editor\_\allowbreak{}planning}: This was one of the most important structure decisions in the chat.\allowbreak{} It prevented the initial implementation from becoming too broad while preserving the long-\allowbreak{}term goal.\allowbreak{} The UI Editor would be a bootstrap tool, not the final architecture.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: The pasted discussion on trees, screws, pottery, axes, chairs, tables, and machines established that the player should manipulate material, geometry, constraints, processes, tools, stations, and affordances.\allowbreak{} The conclusion is that "item classes" must not be the truth substrate.\allowbreak{} Recipes and blueprints can exist, but as higher-\allowbreak{}order formalizations.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Universe\_\allowbreak{}Explorer\_\allowbreak{}Planning}: A major theme was that useful local inventions must become portable, standardized, industrialized, and institutionally adopted.\allowbreak{} The repo now has a Formalization Chain spec and Player Desire Acceptance Map that strongly preserve this.\allowbreak{} The future work is making this playable: drafting, measuring, testing, blueprinting, certifying, teaching, manufacturing, maintaining, and revising designs.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The user noted that earlier prompts were hard to copy because they were not always contained in one code block, and that prompts should better handle dirty worktrees and concurrent tasks.\allowbreak{} From then on, generated prompts included detailed dirty worktree handling, allowed/\allowbreak{}forbidden paths, non-\allowbreak{}goals, validation, blocker classification, and commit/\allowbreak{}final-\allowbreak{}response formats.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{Workbench\_\allowbreak{}AIDE\_\allowbreak{}Product\_\allowbreak{}Spine}: The final recommended sequence was: finish replay proof and barebones client, run product spine review, then begin limited parallel dev.\allowbreak{} Larger parallel waves should wait until minimum AIDE workflow law exists.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: The future relevance of this chat is high.\allowbreak{} It should feed directly into the future project spec book and into a corrected implementation prompt.\allowbreak{} But it should not be treated as final implementation detail everywhere.\allowbreak{} Many high-\allowbreak{}level decisions are settled, but solver formulas, exact file encoding details, build system integration, actual repository layout, and some numerical representations still require verification.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{World\_\allowbreak{}Architecture}: Before reading the rest of this report, remember this: this chat's central contribution is not one isolated feature.\allowbreak{} It is a whole design philosophy for Dominium's world engine.\allowbreak{} The game should be deterministic, fixed-\allowbreak{}point, sparse, modular, content-\allowbreak{}driven, and field-\allowbreak{}based.\allowbreak{} Chunks are not the world.\allowbreak{} Chunks are just how the world is cached, meshed, streamed, and saved.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{xstack\_\allowbreak{}lab\_\allowbreak{}galaxy}: and XStack must serve development rather than development serving XStack.\allowbreak{}
\item \texttt{xstack\_\allowbreak{}lab\_\allowbreak{}galaxy}: After the transcript appeared, the user asked for a maximum-\allowbreak{}fidelity Context Transfer Packet.\allowbreak{} The assistant produced one, with sections such as workstreams, decisions, tasks, constraints, open questions, risks, artifacts, and verification queue.\allowbreak{} Then the user asked to turn that into a downloadable report package.\allowbreak{} The assistant created Markdown/\allowbreak{}YAML files and a ZIP archive.\allowbreak{}
\end{itemize}
